item.title: Trump Implemented Far-Right Republican Policies – And They’re Failing
item.description: As it wreaks enormous damage on America and the world, Donald Trump’s second presidency has in one way performed a service. Again and again, Trump has acted without restraint to indulge his longstanding obsessions and those of the Republican Party that empowers him. His abandon has produced…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
As it wreaks enormous damage on America and the world, Donald Trump’s second presidency has in one way performed a service.

Again and again, Trump has acted without restraint to indulge his longstanding obsessions and those of the Republican Party that empowers him. His abandon has produced real-time tests of familiar arguments they’ve offered for years.

And in one realm after another, they’ve flunked. Let’s call the roll.

For decades, GOP hard-liners have yearned to use American might against Iran and its Islamist theocracy. To their chagrin, presidents of both parties shied away from the risk. When Barack Obama rallied U.S. allies behind a negotiated agreement to limit Iran’s nuclear threat, Republican hawks denounced the deal as a giveaway.

In his first term, Trump scuttled it. In his second, he has gone to war.

Instead of the quick-strike triumph he envisioned, the president has met strategic disaster.

Despite the on-the-ground destruction from a massive U.S. bombing campaign, Iran’s resilient response has strengthened its extremist regime, weakened the global economy, and raised prices for American consumers. Now Trump desperately wants out.

His Iran blunder has simultaneously exposed the fecklessness of the administration’s approach to military affairs more broadly. Fox anchor-turned-Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth, like Trump preoccupied with appearances and bygone social hierarchies, has culled Black and women commanders, demanded “warfighters” with better-sculpted bodies, and made “lethality” his pre-eminent value.

Yet Hegseth’s “warrior ethos” has not achieved U.S. objectives in an era that favors technological resourcefulness over brawn. While the Pentagon burns through its arsenal of heavy weaponry, Iran has thwarted Trump with inexpensive drones – one of which may have just downed an American helicopter near the Strait of Hormuz.

Nor is brute force winning Trump’s drug war. Courting working-class communities anguished by toll of narcotics, Trump in 2024 suggested the military could smash international cartels. Back in office, he has authorized deadly strikes against small boats in the Caribbean allegedly carrying cocaine and fentanyl.

The strikes, gleefully promoted by the administration in social media videos, have summarily executed nearly 200 people without trial. But drug war experts recently told the New York Times they haven’t diminished the availability or even raised the price of cocaine on American streets. A Johns Hopkins professor likened the campaign to bombing a few McDonald’s restaurants and claiming to have curbed consumption of junk food.

The GOP’s swelling white nationalist constituency claims that removing dark-skinned immigrants will benefit native-born U.S. citizens. Yet Trump and his ICE shock troops have demonstrated in Minnesota and elsewhere that “mass deportation” requires more government violence and disruption than the American people will tolerate.

So with midterm elections approaching, the administration has somewhat backed off.

For many years, Trump has claimed that tariffs on imports from abroad would revive American industry. As they imposed large tariffs on “Liberation Day” last year, the president and administration lackeys insisted that foreign countries would foot the bill.

But have they? The Yale Budget Lab has calculated that the price of imports rose by 1.5% during 2025 – boosting the inflation that Trump had promised to eliminate. And if you think the administration doesn’t know that they’ve been taxing American consumers and business, consider: The White House recently announced tariff cuts on agricultural equipment to reduce costs for struggling farmers and manufacturers.

And then there’s all that federal spending fraud that right-wingers have insisted could be excised if only budget officials had the guts. Well, Trump handed Elon Musk and his tech-bro guerrillas a DOGE chainsaw and they couldn’t find it. (DOGE succeeded, however, in cutting a program to monitor the cross-border scourge of New World screwworm, which has now been found in animals in Texas and New Mexico.)

Since Ronald Reagan’s era, Republican supply-siders have predicted that tax cuts would pay for themselves. By making America’s economy take off “like a rocket ship,” as Trump likes to say, tax cuts generate new government revenues from increased growth.

It hasn’t happened. After Trump’s first big tax cut, the federal budget deficit grew as a share of the economy from 3.4% in 2017 to 4.6% in 2019, the White House budget office has reported.

His second, in 2025, will expand the red ink by $4.7 trillion through 2035, the Congressional Budget Office forecasts; the budget deficit will expand to 6.7% of the economy.

One more thing. Anyone who remembers the self-styled Moral Majority, and The Book of Virtues by ex-Reagan Cabinet member William Bennett, knows how Republicans have repeatedly claimed the high ground on values. They’ve cast Democrats as the libertine party of “moral relativism” and disdain for America.

Grading such claims has been subjective and difficult – until now. Considering Trump’s undisguised corruption, dishonesty, contempt for the rule of law, and preference for autocracies over our democratic allies, what do you think?

John Harwood is the former chief Washington correspondent for CNBC and White House correspondent for CNN. He has interviewed every president from George H.W. Bush to Joe Biden. Sign up for ‘The Stakes with John Harwood’ to get all of his columns in your inbox.

item.title: 80 Things to Know About Donald Trump on His 80th Birthday
item.description: To mark Donald Trump’s 80th birthday, here are 80 things you should know about the sitting president of the United States (who will leave office in January 2029 as the oldest president in the history of the country). His father was a real estate mogul Donald Trump’s father, Fred Trump, was a…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
To mark Donald Trump’s 80th birthday, here are 80 things you should know about the sitting president of the United States (who will leave office in January 2029 as the oldest president in the history of the country).

  • His father was a real estate mogul

    • Donald Trump’s father, Fred Trump, was a prominent real estate developer who

      builtmore than 27,000 apartments and row houses across New York City.
  • His mother was an immigrant

    • Mary Anne Trump

      immigratedto the U.S. from Scotland in 1930, having learned English as asecond language.
  • He grew up in a mansion

    • Donald Trump grew up in a 23-room brick

      mansionin the affluent neighborhood of Jamaica Estates, Queens.
  • He became a millionaire at just eight years old

    • According to a

      New York Timesinvestigation, by the age of three, Donald Trump earned today’s equivalent of more than $200,000 a year, making him a millionaire by the age of eight.
  • He was seen as the favorite son

    • Donald Trump was seen as the

      favoriteof the three Trump boys, taking over his eldest brother’s position as the apparent heir to the Trump real estate empire.
  • He does not drink alcohol

    • Speaking of Trump’s eldest brother, Freddy

      developed alcoholismin the 1960s and died of a heart attack at the age of 42. Donald Trumpcites thisas the reason he doesn’t drink.
  • His late sister was a Clinton-appointed judge

    • Maryanne Trump Barry was

      appointedby President Bill Clinton to the U.S. Court of Appeals in 1999, serving in the role for 16 years before retiring amid aninvestigationinto whether she had committed tax fraud. Guess it runs in the family.
  • He was sent to military school

    • Donald Trump was sent to

      the New York Military Academyat the age of 13, where he wasdescribedby many of his classmates as a “bully.” Are we surprised?
  • Yet… he dodged the Vietnam War draft

    • Despite attending military school, Trump did not, in fact, go on to serve in the military during the Vietnam War. He avoided the draft

      five times: the first four were to complete his college degree, and the fifth time, he was diagnosed withbone spursthat would disqualify him from service.
  • He graduated from the University of Pennsylvania’s Wharton School of Business

    • Trump has repeatedly claimed he graduated at the top of his class in 1968, yet, Wharton’s published academic honors and deans lists for that year

      do not mention his name. Awkward.
  • He’s a nepo baby

    • After graduating from the University of Pennsylvania, Trump immediately began working for his father. He became

      presidentof the Trump Organization a whopping three years later.
    Donald Trump and Fred Trump at the Trump Tower Atrium in New York City on Dec. 12, 1987. Photo by Sonia Moskowitz/Getty Images
  • He and his father were sued for discrimination

    • The Justice Department

      suedthe Trumps in 1973 for refusing to rent apartments in predominantly white buildings to Black tenants.
  • But he did not have to admit to using discriminatory practices

    • The case ended in a

      settlementthat did not require the Trumps to explicitly acknowledge that they used discriminatory practices. However, the government’s description of the settlement says they “failed and neglected” to comply with the Fair Housing Act.
  • Roy Cohn was one of his closest friends and mentors

    • Roy Cohn, who once worked for Sen. Joseph McCarthy as a

      “Red Scare” prosecutor, became one of Trump’s closest allies, and the tworeportedlyspoke on the phone up to five times per day.
  • He used Cohn’s mob ties to help build his real estate projects

    • Roy Cohn also just so happened to have

      connections with the mobwhich controlled a large part of New York City’s construction unions. Trump used these mob connections to helpbuildTrump Tower and Trump Plaza.
  • He also relied on undocumented workers

    • To make way for Trump Tower, Trump

      employed200 undocumented Polish workers to demolish the Bonwit Teller building. In true capitalist fashion, he subjected them to 12-hour shifts where they were paid as little as $4 an hour – less than half the union wage.
  • He failed to build a casino empire

    • Trump

      openedthree casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey – all of which later filed for bankruptcy. You can’t win ‘em all.
  • He bought an airline

    • In 1988, Trump

      launched‘Trump Shuttle,’ a fleet of planes and their accompanying routes and terminalspurchasedfrom the failing Eastern Airlines. It was never profitable, and was ultimately sold to U.S. Airways.
    Donald Trump during Launch of Trump Steaks in New York City. Photo by Stephen Lovekin/WireImage for Hill & Knowlton
  • He started a steak brand
  • And a water brand

    • Dubbed ‘Trump Ice,’ Donald Trump’s

      bottled water brandlaunched in 2004 and was also a failure. The good news? If you have an unopened bottle lying around, you can make apretty penny.
  • … And a Vodka brand
  • Oh, and don’t forget, more recently, the Bibles!
  • He’s filed for Chapter 11 Bankruptcy six times

    • After the three Atlantic City casinos declared bankruptcy, the Plaza Hotel, Trump Hotels and Casinos Resorts, and Trump Entertainment Resorts

      alsoall filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy.
  • While Trump’s Atlantic City stint was short-lived, it landed him in the WWE universe

    • The Trump Plaza in Atlantic City NJ

      hostedWrestleMania two years in a row.
  • He even appeared in WWE storylines

    • In

      2007, WWE Chairman Vince McMahonchallengedTrump to a “Battle of the Billionaires” match at Wrestlemania 23. Trump won, and as his prize, heshavedMcMahon’s head in the ring.
    Vince McMahon has his head shaved by Donald Trump and Bobby Lashley at the 2007 Wrestlemania on April 1, 2007 in Detroit, Michigan. Photo by Bill Pugliano/Getty Images
  • He made a cameo in Home Alone 2

    • The Christmas classic was

      filmedin the Plaza Hotel, which only happened underone condition: that its owner, Donald Trump, would be featured in the movie.
  • He had a brief stint as a Broadway producer
  • He became a reality television star

    • Trump hosted ‘The Apprentice’ in 2004, where he would become famous for telling contestants “You’re Fired!”

  • He also became involved in pageants

    • In 1996, Trump became a

      co-ownerof Miss Universe, which also includes Miss USA and Miss Teen USA pageants. We can all see where this is going…
  • He was a known creep among pageant contestants

    • Surprise, surprise! Donald Trump was often found

      lurkingin the dressing rooms, includingMiss Teen USApageants where some of the girls were completely naked.
  • He’s joked that he’d date his own daughter

    • In 2006, Trump

      saidon ABC’s ‘The View,’ that his then 24-year-old daughter Ivanka “does have a very nice figure.” He continued, “I’ve said if Ivanka weren’t my daughter, perhaps I’d be dating her.”
  • He’s been accused of sexual misconduct at least 28 times

    • Yes,

      28 accusations… that we know of. The list includes allegations of forcible kissing, touching, groping, rape, and more. He’s denied all accusations.
  • He’s even bragged about it!

    • In a resurfaced ‘Access Hollywood’ tape from 2005, Trump was caught

      saying, “I’m automatically attracted to beautiful women. I just start kissing them, it’s like a magnet. Just kiss. I don’t even wait. And when you’re a star, they let you do it. You can do anything – Grab ‘em by the pussy.”
  • He was found liable for sexual abuse in a civil trial

    • A jury found Trump

      liablefor sexually abusing writer E. Jean Carroll in 1996, after she alleged that he raped her in the dressing room of a department store.
  • He is the first U.S. president to stand a criminal trial

    • Donald Trump became the first U.S. president to stand

      criminal trialwhen he was charged with 34 counts of falsifying business records in New York.
  • He was found guilty of paying ‘hush money’ to a sex worker

    • In that very same criminal trial, Donald Trump was

      found guiltyof paying adult film star Stormy Daniels $130,000 tokeep quietabout their affair during the 2016 presidential election.
  • He was friends with Jeffrey Epstein

    • Trump and Epstein’s friendship dates back to the late 1980s, where the two would often

      mingleat Trump’s Mar-a-Lago Estate. And according to a birthday card obtained by theHouse Oversight Committee, Trump alluded to a “wonderful secret” they both shared…Photo released by the House Oversight Committee.
  • They bonded over creeping on women

    • In an interview with

      New York Magazine, Trumpsaidthat Epstein, “likes beautiful women as much as I do, and many of them are on the younger side.” Gross.
  • And Trump allegedly groped Epstein’s girlfriend while he watched

    • Former model Stacey Williams, Epstein’s former girlfriend,

      allegedthat Trump groped her while Epstein looked on. Trump denies doing so.
  • He’s even been accused of raping one of his wives

    • Trump’s first wife, Ivana Trump,

      accusedhim of raping her. She later walked it back, saying her words shouldn’t be interpreted in a “literal or criminal sense.”
  • He’s been married three times

    • Trump married

      threedifferent women: model Ivana Trump, actress and singer Marla Maples, and model and First Lady Melania Trump.
  • He has five children from three wives

    • Donald Jr., Ivanka, and Eric with his first wife Ivana; Tiffany with his second wife Marla; and Barron with Melania.

  • And they, too, are nepo babies

    • Ivanka and her husband Jared Kushner were

      senior advisersto Trump during his first term as president. Meanwhile, Eric and Don Jr. stepped in to run the Trump Organization.
  • He wasn’t always a Republican

    • Trump first

      registeredas a Republican in 1969, but later became a member of the Independence Party – an offshoot of the centrist Reform Party – in 1999. Three years later, he would register as a Democrat, before reverting back to Republican in 2009.
  • He asked to be George H.W. Bush’s vice president

    • In 1988, Trump allegedly

      approachedthen RNC chair Lee Atwater to be Bush’s running mate – a request Bush described as “strange and unbelievable.” Trump laterallegedthat Atwater was the one to approachhim.Sure.
  • He ran for president once before the 2016 election

    • In 2000, Trump

      ran for presidentunder the Reform Party but withdrew after failing to secure the nomination.
  • He amplified the Obama ‘birther’ conspiracy

    • As Barack Obama sought reelection in 2011, Donald Trump

      cast doubtonto his citizenship status,allegingthat he was actually born in Kenya, not Hawaii.
    Donald Trump rides an escalator to a press event to announce his candidacy on June 16, 2015 in New York City. Photo by Christopher Gregory/Getty Images
  • He launched his 2016 campaign in the most Trumpian way

    • In June 2015, Donald Trump

      descendeda golden escalator in Trump Tower to officially launch his presidential campaign.
  • He hired paid actors to attend his campaign launch

    • If the golden escalator wasn’t out of touch enough, his campaign literally

      paidactors $50 to “wear t-shirts and carry signs and help cheer him in support of his announcement.”
  • His 2016 run was shadowed by his history of racism

    • While campaigning, Trump faced backlash for his aggressive lobbying for the

      death penaltyagainst the ‘Central Park Five,’ a group of Black and Latino boys who were wrongfully convicted of rape in 1989.
  • His history of fraud also came to light

    • Trump’s opponents, including

      Marco Rubio, slammed him for his role indefrauding studentsat his short-lived real estate school, Trump University. Hesettledthe case for $25 million in 2025.
  • He’s aware of the cult following he’s created

    • During the 2016 campaign trail, Trump

      proclaimed, “I could stand in the middle of Fifth Avenue and shoot somebody, and I wouldn’t lose any voters.”
  • He was the most inexperienced president in U.S. history

    • Trump heavily relied on his cult following to land himself in the White House, becoming the first U.S. president to have

      no priormilitary or political experience.
  • Russia wanted him to win

    • An intelligence community

      investigationrevealed that Russia meddled in the 2016 election and “demonstrated a preference for candidate Trump.”
  • He was the first modern president to not release his tax returns

    • Trump became the first presidential candidate in more than 40 years to not release his

      tax returnsprior to taking office, likely to avoid the fact that he paidvery little.
  • He enforced an insanely cruel ‘child separation’ policy

    • More than 5,000 children were forcibly

      separated(kidnapped?) from their parents at the border during Trump’s first term, many of whom wereheld in cages.
  • He hates the media
  • Except for Fox…

    • Yes, Trump is seemingly

      addictedto watching Fox, a staple channel in his daily dose ofreportedlyat least 4 hours worth of cable news programming.
  • He wrote Kim Jong Un ‘love letters’

    • Trump and the North Korean dictator

      exchangedletters during his first presidency, which were filled with flowery language. Even Trumpdescribedthem as “love letters.”
    Donald Trump removes his mask upon return to the White House from Walter Reed National Military Medical Center on Oct. 5, 2020 in Washington, DC. Photo by Win McNamee/Getty Images
  • He suggested dangerous (non-) cures for Covid infections

    • At the height of the Covid pandemic, Trump

      suggestedinjecting disinfectants like bleach into the body and even exposing infected individuals to UV light.
  • He grossly mishandled the pandemic

    • Trump could have avoided

      hundreds of thousandsof Covid-related deaths in the US if he had implemented earlier mask mandates, social distancing rules, and robust testing guidelines, according tomultiplestudies.
  • He described some white supremacists as “very fine people”

    • After the 2017 neo-Nazi “Unite the Right” rally in Charlottesville, Trump

      describedthe attendees as “very fine people on both sides.” He then clarified that the neo-Nazis and white nationalists should be “condemned totally.”
  • He wanted to ‘shoot’ Black Lives Matter protesters

    • As Black Lives Matter protests broke out in response to George Floyd’s murder, Trump

      reportedlysuggested shooting protesters in the legs to quell unrest.
  • He pressured Ukraine to investigate the Bidens

    • In the lead-up to the 2020 election, Trump reportedly

      urgedUkrainian President Volodymyr Zelensky to launch an investigation into Joe Biden and his son, Hunter. This push came after Trump’s decision to freeze military aid to Ukraine, and was widely regarded as aquid pro quodeal.
  • He tried to overturn the 2020 election

    • As the votes were being counted, Trump falsely claimed the results were rigged against him and,

      among other things,pressuredGeorgia’s secretary of state to reverse the results, citing conspiracies about widespread voter fraud, andaskedhim to “find 11,780 votes” – one more vote than what Biden received.
    Donald Trump poses for his booking photo at the Fulton County Jail on Aug. 24, 2023 in Atlanta, Georgia. Photo by Fulton County Sheriff's Office via Getty Images
  • He became the first president to have to pose for a mug shot

    • Following his attempt to overturn Georgia’s election results, Trump, facing 13 criminal charges,

      posedfor his infamous mugshot.
  • He ‘lit the fire’ of the Jan. 6 insurrection

    • When the election didn’t fall in his favor, a House investigation

      revealedthat Trump encouraged his supporters to take to the Capitol on Jan. 6, telling them to “fight like hell.”
  • And then pardoned the insurrectionists

    • Upon his return to office, Trump

      pardonedthe violent insurrectionists who were indicted or sentenced after the Capitol attack.
  • He was impeached twice

    • Trump is the only U.S. president to be

      impeachedtwice: once for the Ukraine controversy and once for inciting the Jan. 6 insurrection. He wasacquittedby the Senate in both proceedings.
    Stacks of boxes in a bathroom and shower in Donald Trump’s Mar-a-Lago estate in Palm Beach, Florida. Photo by U.S. Department of Justice via Getty Images
  • He stored classified government documents at his home
  • He launched a meme coin days before he took office for the second time

    • The Trump family has

      raked inat least $2.3 billion in profit from their crypto ventures since the beginning of the second administration, according to a Reuters analysis.
  • He’s issued a record number of executive orders

    • In the first 100 days of his presidency, Trump issued

      more executive ordersthan any other U.S. president, covering everything from attacks on DEI programs to establishing DOGE to gut key parts of the government.
  • He’s obsessed with tariffs

    • In the first few months of his second term, Trump unleashed a

      waveof tariffs on nearly every single country – a move that the Supreme Court laterknocked down. But his obsession with tariffs dates back to the 80s, when he called for a 15-20% tax on Japanese imports afterlosing an auctionto a Japanese trading company.
    Donald Trump holds up a chart while speaking during his “Liberation Day” event on April 2, 2025. Photo by Chip Somodevilla/Getty Images
  • He’s pushing a dangerous imperialist agenda

    • Since taking office, Trump has repeatedly threatened to

      annexGreenland and Canada in a bid to revive the era of Americanexpansionism.
  • And he’s already had his hand in regime change

    • Trump

      kidnappedVenezuelan President Nicolás Maduro, took credit forassassinatingIranian Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, and iseyeinga regime change in Cuba.
  • He started an illegal war with Iran

    • Despite

      vowingto not start any new wars, Trump launched an illegal war in Iran with Israel and hascontinued to lieto the American public about it ending.
  • He’s trying to get rid of birthright citizenship

    • In January 2025, Trump signed an

      executive orderto strip birthright citizenship from babies born in the U.S. to non-citizen parents. If upheld by the Supreme Court, roughly 2.7 million people areexpectedto lose their status over the next 20 years.
  • He’s weaponized ICE as his personal militia

    • Trump has cracked down hard on immigration, deploying masked,

      militarizedICE agents to blue cities like Los Angeles and Minneapolis. These ICE agents have evenkilledU.S. citizens in broad daylight.
  • He’s spent more time golfing than any other president

    • When he’s not starting new wars or terrorizing everyday Americans, Trump is often golfing. In fact, he’s spent over 20% of his second term playing golf,

      costingtaxpayers an estimated $155 million.
  • He’s not ruling out a third term

    • Yes, you read that right. Trump has repeatedly

      suggestedthat he’s weighing an unconstitutional third term, which he would begin at the ripe age of 82.

item.title: This Week in Palestine: Israel Kills 8-Year-Old Schoolboy and 15-Year-Old Fisherman Just Days After Murdering 7-Mo…
item.description: Not long after we wrapped up last week’s edition of ‘This Week in Palestine,’ we started to hear reports that Israeli forces had killed a 7-month-old boy the previous Friday in the occupied West Bank. His name was Sam Fahd Abu Haikal , a baby, shot in the face. Sam’s killing was barely a blimp in…
item.author: This Week in Palestine | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Not long after we wrapped up last week’s edition of ‘This Week in Palestine,’ we started to hear reports that Israeli forces had killed a 7-month-old boy the previous Friday in the occupied West Bank. His name was Sam Fahd Abu Haikal, a baby, shot in the face.

Sam’s killing was barely a blimp in the Western media news cycle when it happened. There was no worldwide outrage. Perhaps that’s because Western media, governments, and politicians like to take Israel at its word: the Israeli military, after all, claimed its soldiers “perceived” the vehicle Sam and his family were traveling in “accelerating toward them.”

What then, when video was later released showing the exact opposite? Shouldn’t there have been worldwide outrage then? As we know all too well, Israel kills children with impunity. Even when there are witnesses, and video, and photos, even when Israel is caught in yet another lie, even when those killed are babies, the world barely bats an eye.

Sam wasn’t the only child killed by Israel over the last week or so. At least three others were killed in Gaza. At the same time, settler violence in the occupied West Bank continued to escalate, and a well-known Gaza doctor held by Israeli without charge for nearly two years appeared via videolink for the first time in over a year.

Needless to say, it was another troubling week in Palestine:

Israeli settlers and at least one soldier rampaged through the occupied West Bank town of Huwara, beating Palestinian residents, smashing car windows, and stealing livestock, according to Palestinian media. At least nine Palestinians were injured in just the latest settler attack in the West Bank.

Israeli forces killed 15-year-old Mohammed Musa Abu Giab after the Israeli Navy opened fire on his and other fishing boats on the coast of Deir al-Balah in central Gaza. At least four other fishermen were reportedly detained.

Al Jazeera, citing Gaza’s Fishermen’s Syndicate, reported that Israel has killed more than 235 Palestinian fishermen in Gaza since the genocide began.

Abu Giab was one of 13 people killed on Sunday, according to news reports. At least one other child was killed in those strikes.

Israel killed at least eight people in Gaza, including 8-year-old Jad Suleiman as he returned home from school in Jabaliya refugee camp in northern Gaza. He was one of seven Palestinians reportedly killed in Israeli strikes on Monday.

The strikes on Sunday and Monday brought the death toll from Israeli attacks since the so-called “ceasefire” took effect to more than 970.

A UN inquiry found what we’ve long known: Israeli authorities have been directly involved in settler attacks that have harmed and displaced Palestinians in the occupied West Bank, and through financial and military support, the state has enabled such violence to rise.

“Violence by settlers is the direct outcome of Israeli policies that support, enable and protect their actions, whereas Hamas-affiliated forces have exploited the vacuum created by relentless Israeli attacks and widespread destruction of Gaza,” said Srinivasan Muralidhar, Chair of the Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory.

Dr. Hussam Abu Safiya, who was abducted from Gaza in December 2024, appeared briefly by video in the Israeli Supreme Court. It was the first time he had been seen publicly in over a year.

He was visibly thinner. His family, lawyers, and former detainees had previously said he had lost more than 88 lbs, been tortured, and suffered health conditions, including scabies.

Haaretz

The new outpost is in the occupied West Bank city of Jenin, where the Israeli military displaced thousands when it launched a military operation there and in Tulkarm and Nur al-Shams in January 2025. Many homes were occupied by Israeli forces and some were later demolished.

The Israeli military said the outpost was supposed to help create conditions that would lead to the eventual withdrawal from the camp, but Haaretz wrote, “sources familiar with developments on the ground told Haaretz that the army outpost is also likely intended to help protect Israeli settlers who are returning to live in the Jenin area.”

A UK judge sentenced four Palestine Action activists to jail terms of roughly five years to eight years, saying their criminal damage conviction over a raid on the Bristol site of the Israeli arms company Elbit Systems in August 2024 had a “terrorist connection.”

Middle East Eye wrote, “This marks the first time in British legal history that the [terrorist] designation has been applied to direct action protesters who were not convicted of terrorist offences or of causing intentional violence.”

Amnesty International warned the decision “risks marking a new low in the ongoing crackdown against protest across the UK.”

Miss last week’s edition? Catch up here. Plus, check out more from Zeteo:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: 7 Things to Know About the Data Center Projects Taking Over the U.S.
item.description: There are now more than 3,000 data centers operational in the United States and more than 1,500 on the way , and the rate at which they’re being built isn’t stopping anytime soon. Data centers now make up 2.3% of total U.S. construction spending , and as artificial intelligence companies race to…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
There are now more than 3,000 data centers operational in the United States and more than 1,500 on the way, and the rate at which they’re being built isn’t stopping anytime soon. Data centers now make up 2.3% of total U.S. construction spending, and as artificial intelligence companies race to build even more warehouses for the servers that power large language models, Americans have noticed, and they’re not particularly happy. A Gallup poll released in May found that 7 in 10 Americans oppose a data center being built near them, with nearly half strongly opposed. These complaints aren’t partisan, with a plurality of Democrats, independents, and Republicans alike opposed to their local construction.

Companies like Amazon, Microsoft, Google, Meta, and Oracle – plus the Blackstone Group, which calls itself the largest investor in AI infrastructure in the world – pitch new jobs and tax revenue to communities that instead end up getting higher electric bills, drained water, lost farmland, and a grating mechanical hum. And last summer, President Donald Trump signed an executive order to fast-track permitting, pushing agencies to ease the very rules built to protect them.

Here are seven things to know about who pays for the data-center buildout – and who’s profiting:

In May, officials in Box Elder County, Utah, cleared the way for the Stratos Project, an AI complex that could eventually draw up to 9 gigawatts of power, which is more than double the roughly 4 gigawatts the entire state of Utah uses in total on average. Box Elder Residents filed three separate referendum petitions to stop it. All three were denied. Outside of Utah, in Wisconsin, there are now two planned projects that would need about 3.9 gigawatts, which is enough to power 4.3 million homes, set inside a state with only 2.8 million housing units in total.

As data centers expand and cluster, they continue to need more energy from their neighboring electrical grid to meet demand, and utility companies have a habit of spreading the increased rate across everyone’s bill. Residential electricity rates rose about 32% nationally from 2020 to 2025, and the buildout of data centers is a growing driver. PJM’s independent market monitor, the watchdog for the grid serving much of the eastern U.S., has named hyperscale data centers the primary driver of soaring regional capacity costs. A few states have started making data centers pay for the energy strain they cause – the obvious fix, which even some in the industry now claim to back. Most haven’t. So the bill for powering someone else’s AI ambitions quietly lands in your mailbox – and good luck finding the line item that explains why.

Nationally, it’s true that data centers use far less water than farms or golf courses. A data center evaporates 70-90% of what it withdraws, versus the 5-15% a typical household consumes, and where farms and golf courses spread their thirst across wide acreage and shifting seasons, a data center sinks a single, constant, often-unpredictable and spiky straw into one town’s system, so the local hit can be staggering. One Meta facility in Newton County, Georgia, uses about 10% of the entire county’s water.

There’s also the noise from the center’s cooling fans and generators that release a relentless low-frequency hum that researchers have linked to sleep loss, chronic stress, and higher blood pressure, to a point that residents in Michigan and New Jersey have begun filing suit.

On top of the bills and the water, taxpayers are often footing the cost for the incentives used to lure these projects in. At least 38 states offer some form of sales-tax break to data centers, pitched as economic development. In Ohio, those exemptions cost the state nearly $1.6 billion in 2025, against an official projection of only $136 million. Within days of those figures surfacing, Republican Governor Mike DeWine unexpectedly paused the breaks while a state committee studies whether these data centers are worth it.

To get online fast, many data centers don’t wait to be connected to their local grid. Instead, some are burning fossil fuels on-site, with diesel generators and gas turbines. Harvard researchers found that a single gas-and-diesel-powered facility in Loudoun County, Virginia, could cause 3.4 to 6.5 premature deaths a year and up to $99 million in annual health damage.

Nationally, one study projects the data-center buildout could cause as many as 1,300 premature deaths a year by 2030 – with a total public health burden expected to double that of the U.S. steel industry and rival deaths caused by every car, bus, and truck in California. The most brazen case south of Memphis, Elon Musk’s xAI ran dozens of gas turbines without air permits to power its Colossus data center. The NAACP sued under the Clean Air Act — and the Trump Justice Department signaled it might intervene on xAI’s side.

The buildout of these centers needs land as much as they need power, and these companies are increasingly taking it from farmers. Across the rural Midwest and South, data centers are converting prime farmland that, once paved, rarely returns to agriculture, while bidding up land far beyond what crops could ever justify – which prices out the people farming it. Wiring it all up means running high-voltage lines through private property: Georgia Power’s $16 billion grid expansion threatens hundreds of properties, with some homes facing demolition and eminent domain hanging over the holdouts. And communities often can’t see it coming – in Virginia, a review found 80% of localities with data centers had signed non-disclosure agreements, with developers using shell companies to mask who’s buying up the neighborhood. Outside Virginia, the same playbook of NDAs and anonymous shell companies has surfaced in Kentucky, Louisiana, and Pennsylvania.

The Trump administration has made its loyalties clear in favor of these companies and not the 7 in 10 Americans who don’t want these data centers anywhere near their homes. These builds reward the tech billionaires who bankrolled Donald Trump’s return, but the president also has a direct financial stake in the fight for nationwide data center expansion.

Trump Media & Technology Group, the parent company of Truth Social, in which Donald Trump holds a roughly 41% stake, announced a $6 billion merger with a fusion-power firm to power the chips in the data centers that the AI boom runs on. That merger was also unveiled the same day regulators let data centers plug directly into power plants. A former White House ethics lawyer called it a serious conflict of interest. In addition to their father, Donald Trump Jr. and Eric Trump invested in an AI data center startup of their own, weeks after the president pledged hundreds of billions to expand AI infrastructure while loosening regulations on the industry.

Zeteo’s Andrew McDonald and Kennedy Garmong contributed to this piece.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: You Can Sell MAGA a Forever War if You Make It a Civilizational War
item.description: The Iran war, supposed to last a mere four weeks, is about halfway through its fourth month. To Donald Trump, the war might be in its third millennium. Trump reached far back into history last weekend as he vocally – and futilely – urged Israel to forestall retaliation against Iranian missiles…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
The Iran war, supposed to last a mere four weeks, is about halfway through its fourth month. To Donald Trump, the war might be in its third millennium.

Trump reached far back into history last weekend as he vocally – and futilely – urged Israel to forestall retaliation against Iranian missiles launched on Tel Aviv to avenge the Israeli bombing of Beirut. In doing so, he backdated the war to long, long before the establishment of the State of Israel, or even the United States of America.

Iran’s missiles “were attacks that did not kick at all,” he told the Financial Times’s Edward Luce. “It’s one of those things that’s been going for 3,000 years, or 47 years, depending on how you count.” To Axios’s Barak Ravid, Trump forecasted that “If Bibi strikes them back it’s just gonna keep going like the last 47 years, or the last 3,000 years.”

We’ll come back to “47 years” in a moment. But claiming Iran has a track record of 3,000 years of aggression is not a statement about history.

It’s a way to square the circle of Trump’s new forever war with his base’s supposed antipathy to them. His formula is to make it a war of civilizational dominance. Trump isn’t really starting new wars, according to this line of thinking. He’s resolving intractable problems, challenges that only a great man of history would dare cut through. This line of thinking seeks to awe you into not noticing that it costs way more to fill up your car, your paycheck doesn’t stretch as far as it did in February, and no one knows what will come of this latest promise of diplomatic resolution.

It’s beside the point to fact-check Trump’s history, but this is what columns are for. The people who speak ancient Iranian, known as the Medes and the Persians, are first mentioned in an Assyrian document from the 9th century BCE that describes them as not aggressors but tributaries. The historian Michael Axworthy records that by the 8th century BCE, they were “fighting back, attacking Assyrian territories.” Then as now, the progenitors of what would become Persian civilization were defending themselves against a more powerful foe.

In the 6th century BCE, Cyrus the Great, who subordinated the Medes to Persia and declared himself king, made Persia a great power. Trump’s formulation, in the context of Israel, implies eternal hostility between Persians and Jews. That’s almost the exact opposite of the true history between both peoples. There have been Jewish communities in Iran for 2,500 years. The era known as the Babylonian Exile, which brought Jews to Iran, was enormously influential on Jewish history, to the point of adopting the local script into what became Hebrew. And in any event, it was Cyrus who ended the Babylonian Exile and permitted the return of the Jews to Palestine – not that we should conflate the modern State of Israel with the ancient Kingdom of Judea, the Second Temple Period, or the Jewish people more broadly. I suspect what Trump is after is to conjure in the minds of his supporters the Persian Empire depicted in the Zach Snyder movie ‘300,’ the one where Gerard Butler’s Leonidas yells “This is Sparta” and kicks a weird-looking Persian envoy down a well.

Portraying a war as the latest flare-up of an ancient conflict is an old trick, especially for Westerners in the Middle East. It obscures the material origins of the war and the perfidies of its aggressors. Palestinians are well accustomed to this sort of thing. The Christian Zionist senator, Lindsey Graham (R-S.C.), in a word salad of manipulated history dressed in neoconservative fantasy, told Fox in March that the war represented “the best chance in 2,000 years, when Iran is neutralized, for Saudi [Arabia] and Israel to recognize each other, end the Arab-Israeli conflict.”

Leave aside Graham’s nonsensical causal connection between the Iran war and regional harmony. Neither Israel nor Saudi Arabia – nor, for that matter, Islam – existed 2,000 years ago. There was no “Arab-Israeli” – by which Graham means, risibly, Arab-Jewish – conflict until Zionist settlement in the late 19th and early 20th centuries stole Palestinian land and cleansed it of Palestinians to ultimately create the State of Israel in 1948. Obscuring the material basis of a war permits perfidious actors, like Graham and Trump, to refuse to redress the material basis of the so-called “Arab-Israeli conflict,” in the hope of substituting U.S. and Israeli regional domination as its outcome. This is the objective of the Abraham Accords that Trump recently floated as a postwar status quo.

It’s also why Trump often prefers to talk about the U.S.-Iran war beginning “47 years” ago, a talking point of many right-wing war supporters shortly after the U.S. and Israel started bombing Iran in late February. Treating the war as 47 years old locates its origins in the 1979 Islamic Revolution. Its logic is that the war began when the Islamic Republic began – and quickly seized U.S. diplomats and their staff as hostages – and will only end with regime change. There is another purpose to starting the clock in 1979. It obscures that the 1979 revolution resulted from popular fury at 25 years of U.S.-backed oppression that began in 1953, when a CIA coup installed a puppet, Mohammad Reza Pahlavi, and ousted the Iranian prime minister who had nationalized Iran’s oil. If Iran has been at war with the United States for 47 years, then the United States has been at war with Iran for 73. But in the sepia-toned, Reagan-filled memories of older conservatives, Iran is eternally unfinished business. Trump hopes to draw on that sentiment to make his war as politically frictionless as possible.

There is an enormous difference between saying the war is 47 years old and saying it is 3,000 years old. It is not principally a difference that concerns time. If the war is 47 years old, the Islamic Republic is the enemy. If the war is 3,000 years old, Iranian civilization is the enemy. And more often than not, Trump has treated it that way. After assassinating Iranian external-security architect Qassem Soleimani in 2020, Trump posted that he had a list of 52 Iranian heritage sites, “important to Iran & the Iranian culture,” that he would bomb if Iran’s reprisal was more than symbolic. Trump set that destruction into motion this year. U.S. bombs didn’t just incinerate more than 100 children at the girls’ school in Minab, they damaged the centuries-old Golestan Palace in Tehran, and this week struck two reservoirs that left 20,000 people briefly without water. Even the Iranian exiles who urged Trump and Netanyahu to end the Islamic Republic were shocked to hear Trump threaten in April that “a whole civilization will die tonight.” They learned what collaborators with imperialism often do: their supposed liberators see them as the useful idiots of an inferior civilization, easily sacrificed, along with everything they cherish.

Trump lost his war early. He lost it once the Iranians closed the Strait of Hormuz, exposing the U.S.’s unexpected lack of coercive power to reopen a globally vital energy and commercial waterway. Trump has flailed ever since. The president careens between empty assurances of diplomatic settlement intended to manipulate markets; boasts about having already bested Iran, no matter how conspicuous Iran’s potency is; and naked bloodthirst posted from the safety of the Oval Office. All the while, energy prices have spiked and dragged inflation up with it. His influencer and journalist defenders are disillusioned by the war – though they have only themselves to blame for their credulity in believing Trump anything but a man of blood. On June 3, sufficient Republican defections permitted the House of Representatives to finally pass a resolution demanding to put the war to a vote.

Trump’s civilizational rhetoric is no less a ploy than his attempts to fool the stock market into thinking peace is on the horizon and all this pain will be over soon. To sustain his war, he is gambling on an instinct among his base that he shares and amplifies. It’s the one that portrays nonwhite immigration as an “invasion.” It’s on display with every ICE raid through marginalized neighborhoods, with every unhinged declaration of dominion over “our hemisphere,“ and with every Department of Homeland Security post containing white supremacist messaging. When a regime metes out violence and humiliation against those considered foreign and inferior, it need never provide for the material improvement of its people.

“I don’t think about Americans’ financial situation,” Trump said last month when asked about the financial impact of his war. Whatever comes of the latest assurance that an interim deal is imminent, Trump seeks to make his base not think of it either. When some people have a cultivated hate in their hearts, they can ignore the pain in their stomachs. Those stomachs can even seem full when told that an adversary’s stomach is emptier. Trump is betting that’s the most powerful characteristic of American civilization.

Spencer Ackerman is a Pulitzer Prize and National Magazine Award-winning reporter and the author of Reign of Terror: How The 9/11 Era Destabilized America and Produced Trump. He is also the author of the FOREVER WARS newsletter on Ghost.

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: 🙈 Trump Still Can’t Admit Iran Kicked His Ass
item.description: On this day in 2016, 49 people were killed by a gunman in the Pulse LGBTQ nightclub in Orlando, Florida. Last year, at the direction of the Trump administration, state authorities painted over a rainbow crosswalk that memorialized the dead. Happy Friday (finally!) Swin here. Today, we’re capping…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 2016, 49 people were killed by a gunman in the Pulse LGBTQ nightclub in Orlando, Florida. Last year, at the direction of the Trump administration, state authorities painted over a rainbow crosswalk that memorialized the dead.

Happy Friday (finally!) Swin here. Today, we’re capping yet another week of our clownishly monarchical, criminal president treating his war in Iran like some sort of demented game show. Like so much else that defines the Trump-Vance era, it would be funny if millions of lives and the global economy weren’t being put in danger, just so the president of the United States can feel like a big man.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we’ll take a look at the simple reason why Donald Trump’s war has devolved into such a muddled Orwellian mess: this president simply won’t concede the fact that he got his ass handed to him by a less powerful, much smaller country. Plus, Trump really does want his birthday to be a national holiday, and some genius stamped “86 47” into the grass of the National Mall.

At the heart of the Pentagon Papers scandal was the fact that the U.S. government, across Democratic and Republican administrations, knew the war in Vietnam was lost – but calculated that they couldn’t tell the American people just yet. In the service of political convenience, the government was willing to throw away ungodly sums of lives and dollars until, years later, they finally couldn’t get away with it any longer.

With their bloodbath in the Middle East, Donald Trump and his Republican Party are performing an aggressively brain-rotted version of that 1970s scandal. One key difference, however, is that the Vietnam War enjoyed American public support for at least some of its duration, and people here thought we could win it. Trump’s war has been disastrously unpopular since day one, and few Americans have ever seen a meaningful victory in reach.

But just like the leaders before him who dragged the United States into an unwinnable war of atrocity and degradation in Indochina, President Trump is refusing to admit what most of his compatriots know: He lost, and there’s no sugar-coating it (but, boy, will Team Trump try).

This week’s events are a perfect example of what happens when the world’s most chronically dunderheaded warmonger can’t just admit he got wrecked by a much smaller adversary.

The bombing war between Iran and the U.S. and Israel ramped back up in recent days, once again collapsing a joke of a ceasefire that was never a ceasefire at all. By Thursday morning, Trump was busy posting about escalating his death-from-above campaign “TONIGHT.” He also threatened to send U.S. troops to invade Iran and “take” Kharg Island. Several close advisers to Trump tell me they believed they talked him out of launching a Kharg Island invasion months ago, only for Trump to fly into a rage and bring it back up this week. (U.S. intelligence and military analysts have repeatedly warned Trump brass that such an invasion would almost certainly result in a slaughter of Americans.) Senior U.S. officials keep telling me that until the war truly ends, the chances of Trump ordering some type of invasion with troops remain uncomfortably high.

“The chances of it change by the week,” says one official. “We think he’s going to, but then he doesn’t.”

By Thursday afternoon, Trump had called it all off… for now, at least. The president, as he’s done so many times before, tried to spin this as progress.

He didn’t concede that he was backing off because the war is destroying him and his party domestically and he desperately wants an off-ramp. He suggested he did so because a glorious peace deal was imminent, a vow he’s falsely made several times before, in the hopes that the American public will buy his bumbling improv routine as the mark of a master negotiator.

Almost instantly after Trump spoke, officials in Israel and Iran began pushing back on his triumphant claims, a maddeningly boring custom in Trump and Benjamin Netanyahu’s hellish conflict.

If recent history is any guide, Trump is very likely just bullshitting his way through this news cycle. If the pattern holds, officials in Tehran will continue to make demands they know Trump would see as red-line-crossing humiliations, all the while knowing what leverage they hold and that they can “ [Jimmy] Carter him” and the Republican Party all the way up to the midterm elections if they want. The Trump administration will keep lumbering back and forth between figuring out a face-saving way to cry uncle, and figuring out how to inflict

And as the powers that be continue rinsing and repeating, incalculable numbers of families and civilians will suffer, all so the former host of NBC’s ‘Celebrity Apprentice’ can have his ego spared from two of the simplest words in the English language: “I lost.”

Whenever this miserable war actually ends, someone will be the last person to die in it. Whoever that person is will at least be spared knowing that their unnecessary, pointless death helped make President Donald Trump feel just a little bit better about himself.

  • TACO Thursday:Donald Trumppromiseda third day of strikes and threatened to seize the Kharg Island oil hub but then backed off, saying talks to end the war were approved at the “highest level” of Iranian leadership.
  • Deal or no deal:Trump then said an agreement to end the war would be signed “very soon, maybe over the weekend in Europe,” with Vice President JD Vance to attend. Trump also said “Iran will never have a nuclear weapon, which is the whole purpose of what we had to go through to get this.”
  • Interested parties:Benjamin Netanyahu’s officesaidIsrael was not party to any U.S.-Iran agreement. Esmail Baghaei, spokesperson for Iran’s Foreign Ministry, said the deal was “mostly finalized” but added: “The problem is that the contradictions in America’s position has caused turbulence to this process.”
  • Dire Strait:Iran said the Strait of Hormuz was still closed. The U.S.saidit wasn’t. Then Trump said it would open once his deal was signed.
  • Damage report:Indiademandedan end to U.S. strikes on ships in its blockade of Iranian ports, after three Indians were killed Wednesday and another ship was attacked Thursday. An Iranian attack caused serious damage to radar facilities and air traffic equipment at Kuwait International Airport, authoritiessaid.
  • Lebanon latest:As Israel pounded southern Lebanon, the official Lebanese news agencyreportedsix people were killed, and several were injured. The Lebanese Health Ministryputthe cumulative death toll at at least 3,711, with more than 11,480 wounded.

This Sunday is President Trump’s 80th birthday. His big day will see UFC fights on the White House lawn, which Trump and the GOP have been hyping for months… and all they have to show for it is an event somehow significantly less popular than Trump’s war on Iran.

As the birthday boy gears up for his party, we feel compelled to remind you of a remarkably sad fact: Yes, President Trump really does want to make his birthday a federal holiday. Whether he ever makes it happen, he’s got some Republican allies on Capitol Hill who would clearly like to help him pull it off.

As Zeteo reported in March:

Sources close to the president have toldZeteothat on separate occasions over the past year, Trump has talked to longtime advisers about what it would take to officially make his birthday a national, federal holiday. Similarly, he has already repeatedly inquired about getting a Donald Trump-shaped monument built in our nation’s capital, and mentioned that he wants final approval on the size, look, location, content, and design of the statue and memorial.

Additionally, two sources familiar with the situation tell me that Trump has casually talked to some GOP lawmakers on Capitol Hill about, as one source dubs it, “the birthday thing.”

It would be deeply weird for a political party to fortify this kind of personality cult for a president whose approval rating hovered at the 60s, as President Barack Obama’s did when he left office and Trump took over the first time. It is much, much more surreal and disturbing for a party to be doing this for a guy whose numbers are stuck in the low-to-mid 30s. It is more deranged than if Hollywood super-agents mounted a serious awards-season push to get ‘Freddy Got Fingered’ an Oscar.

And yet here we are! Happy birthday, I guess?

  • DNI pick:Donald TrumpnamedJay Clayton, a former SEC chair now US attorney in Manhattan, to be director of national intelligence. Trump’s choice of acting DNI, housing official and MAGA warrior Bill Pulte, stoked a congressional rebellion, which means a key foreign surveillance authority is set to lapse Friday.
  • Unimpeachable cheek:Trump and allies have discussed pushing Congress to pass a resolution voiding his two first-term impeachments, theWall Street Journalscooped. Such a move would be of symbolic value only.
  • Penta-gone:The Senate Armed Services Committeevotedbehind closed doors to rename the Department of Defense as the Department of War. Democratic Senator Tim Kaine called the move “juvenile,” but with the House in line, the change is set to become law.
  • Bout bucks:Trump’s White House UFC fight on Sunday has cost “well over $60 million,” the administrationsaidin a filing against an attempt to block the event.
  • Numbers game:On the National Mall, someoneetchedinto the grass the numbers “86 47.” Former FBI Director James Comey is under indictment for posting a photo of seashells spelling out those numbers, because “86” is restaurant slang for getting rid of something, and Trump is the 47th president.
  • ICE flow:Republicans arepreparinga third bill to send massive funding to ICE and Border Patrol, which have already received a quarter of a trillion dollars.
  • Thrown to the sharks:The Trump administrationwantspeople who can’t pay their medical bills to take loans from… health insurance companies.
  • UM arrests:One of eight peopleindictedfor allegedly threatening University of Michigan leaders while mounting pro-Palestinian protests was, until recently, a staffer for Democratic U.S. Senate hopeful Abdul El-Sayed.

Which president, while boxing in the White House gym, took a blow that damaged the sight in his left eye, causing him to take up jiu-jitsu instead?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israeli strikes killed three, health officialssaid, as talks continued between mediators, Hamas, and other Palestinian factions about how to implement the second phase of the supposed ceasefire brokered by the U.S. last year. Hamas called for Israel to be compelled to stop violating the truce.
  • 🇨🇺 New sanctions:Announcingsanctions against Cuba’s state-owned oil and gas company, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio did not pause to consider the evident irony as he accused Havana of weaponizing energy supply.

  • 🇬🇧

    Labour pains:As PM Keir Starmer facedracist rioting in Northern Irelandand a Lancashire election that could send likely challenger Andy Burnham back to Parliament, Defense Secretary John Healeyunexpectedly quit, saying Britain was not spending enough on its military “at this dangerous time.”

  • 🇸🇩

    Sudan strikes:Drone attacks by paramilitary forceskilledat least 15 and wounded dozens in central Sudan, authorities said. At least 59,000 people have been killed since the army and RSF paramilitaries first clashed in April 2023.


Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins doesn’t exactly instill confidence that the Trump administration knows how to protect the Texas cattle industry against the deadly screwworm. She does have Dr. Sean Conley, formerly Donald Trump’s personal doctor, and some sort of a plan to “put AI on the drones.” Presumably it’s “sharks with frickin’ laser beams” next.

Aaron Schock’s Pursuit of a Second Chance — and a Gold Mine

Aaron Schock was once a cautionary tale: a Republican congressman from Illinois, he denied reports he was gay while voting against gay rights, spent lavishly on travel and decor, then resigned for misusing public funds. Now, having come out in 2020, he’s prepping another such tale, concerning Venezuelan gold and the absurdities of Trump’s Washington. For Politico, Adam Wren and Eric Bazail-Eimil tell it well.

Another week, another tale of staggering Trump corruption. For ProPublica, Joshua Kaplan, Justin Elliott, and Alex Mierjeski show how an Indian billionaire bought off the administration by pouring cash into an obscure start-up linked to Donald Trump Jr. If such tales are beginning to seem familiar, the corruption they detail does not get any less staggering, nor does the necessity of such reporting lessen even one iota.

Many Democrats and Republicans Agree That Platner Must Go

…but Sebastian Junger, author of The Perfect Storm, War, and other bestsellers, is not so sure. On his Substack, Junger follows up an account of breakfast with the Democratic Maine Senate candidate with a consideration of events prominently including the New York Times hit piece in which a Republican activist who dated Platner sought to cast doubt on his character. Junger isn’t endorsing Platner, but he is distinctly unimpressed with the candidate’s detractors on both sides of the aisle.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

  • Deal or no deal:Trump then said an agreement to end the war would be signed “very soon, maybe over the weekend in Europe,” with Vice President JD Vance to attend. Trump also said “Iran will never have a nuclear weapon, which is the whole purpose of what we had to go through to get this.”
  • Interested parties:Benjamin Netanyahu’s officesaidIsrael was not party to any U.S.-Iran agreement. Esmail Baghaei, spokesperson for Iran’s Foreign Ministry, said the deal was “mostly finalized” but added: “The problem is that the contradictions in America’s position has caused turbulence to this process.”
  • Dire Strait:Iran said the Strait of Hormuz was still closed. The U.S.saidit wasn’t. Then Trump said it would open once his deal was signed.
  • Damage report:Indiademandedan end to U.S. strikes on ships in its blockade of Iranian ports, after three Indians were killed Wednesday and another ship was attacked Thursday. An Iranian attack caused serious damage to radar facilities and air traffic equipment at Kuwait International Airport, authoritiessaid.
  • Lebanon latest:As Israel pounded southern Lebanon, the official Lebanese news agencyreportedsix people were killed, and several were injured. The Lebanese Health Ministryputthe cumulative death toll at at least 3,711, with more than 11,480 wounded.

This Sunday is President Trump’s 80th birthday. His big day will see UFC fights on the White House lawn, which Trump and the GOP have been hyping for months… and all they have to show for it is an event somehow significantly less popular than Trump’s war on Iran.

As the birthday boy gears up for his party, we feel compelled to remind you of a remarkably sad fact: Yes, President Trump really does want to make his birthday a federal holiday. Whether he ever makes it happen, he’s got some Republican allies on Capitol Hill who would clearly like to help him pull it off.

As Zeteo reported in March:

Sources close to the president have toldZeteothat on separate occasions over the past year, Trump has talked to longtime advisers about what it would take to officially make his birthday a national, federal holiday. Similarly, he has already repeatedly inquired about getting a Donald Trump-shaped monument built in our nation’s capital, and mentioned that he wants final approval on the size, look, location, content, and design of the statue and memorial.

Additionally, two sources familiar with the situation tell me that Trump has casually talked to some GOP lawmakers on Capitol Hill about, as one source dubs it, “the birthday thing.”

It would be deeply weird for a political party to fortify this kind of personality cult for a president whose approval rating hovered at the 60s, as President Barack Obama’s did when he left office and Trump took over the first time. It is much, much more surreal and disturbing for a party to be doing this for a guy whose numbers are stuck in the low-to-mid 30s. It is more deranged than if Hollywood super-agents mounted a serious awards-season push to get ‘Freddy Got Fingered’ an Oscar.

And yet here we are! Happy birthday, I guess?

  • DNI pick:Donald TrumpnamedJay Clayton, a former SEC chair now US attorney in Manhattan, to be director of national intelligence. Trump’s choice of acting DNI, housing official and MAGA warrior Bill Pulte, stoked a congressional rebellion, which means a key foreign surveillance authority is set to lapse Friday.
  • Unimpeachable cheek:Trump and allies have discussed pushing Congress to pass a resolution voiding his two first-term impeachments, theWall Street Journalscooped. Such a move would be of symbolic value only.
  • Penta-gone:The Senate Armed Services Committeevotedbehind closed doors to rename the Department of Defense as the Department of War. Democratic Senator Tim Kaine called the move “juvenile,” but with the House in line, the change is set to become law.
  • Bout bucks:Trump’s White House UFC fight on Sunday has cost “well over $60 million,” the administrationsaidin a filing against an attempt to block the event.
  • Numbers game:On the National Mall, someoneetchedinto the grass the numbers “86 47.” Former FBI Director James Comey is under indictment for posting a photo of seashells spelling out those numbers, because “86” is restaurant slang for getting rid of something, and Trump is the 47th president.
  • ICE flow:Republicans arepreparinga third bill to send massive funding to ICE and Border Patrol, which have already received a quarter of a trillion dollars.
  • Thrown to the sharks:The Trump administrationwantspeople who can’t pay their medical bills to take loans from… health insurance companies.
  • UM arrests:One of eight peopleindictedfor allegedly threatening University of Michigan leaders while mounting pro-Palestinian protests was, until recently, a staffer for Democratic U.S. Senate hopeful Abdul El-Sayed.

Which president, while boxing in the White House gym, took a blow that damaged the sight in his left eye, causing him to take up jiu-jitsu instead?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Israeli strikes killed three, health officialssaid, as talks continued between mediators, Hamas, and other Palestinian factions about how to implement the second phase of the supposed ceasefire brokered by the U.S. last year. Hamas called for Israel to be compelled to stop violating the truce.
  • 🇨🇺 New sanctions:Announcingsanctions against Cuba’s state-owned oil and gas company, U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio did not pause to consider the evident irony as he accused Havana of weaponizing energy supply.

  • 🇬🇧

    Labour pains:As PM Keir Starmer facedracist rioting in Northern Irelandand a Lancashire election that could send likely challenger Andy Burnham back to Parliament, Defense Secretary John Healeyunexpectedly quit, saying Britain was not spending enough on its military “at this dangerous time.”

  • 🇸🇩

    Sudan strikes:Drone attacks by paramilitary forceskilledat least 15 and wounded dozens in central Sudan, authorities said. At least 59,000 people have been killed since the army and RSF paramilitaries first clashed in April 2023.


Agriculture Secretary Brooke Rollins doesn’t exactly instill confidence that the Trump administration knows how to protect the Texas cattle industry against the deadly screwworm. She does have Dr. Sean Conley, formerly Donald Trump’s personal doctor, and some sort of a plan to “put AI on the drones.” Presumably it’s “sharks with frickin’ laser beams” next.

Aaron Schock’s Pursuit of a Second Chance — and a Gold Mine

Aaron Schock was once a cautionary tale: a Republican congressman from Illinois, he denied reports he was gay while voting against gay rights, spent lavishly on travel and decor, then resigned for misusing public funds. Now, having come out in 2020, he’s prepping another such tale, concerning Venezuelan gold and the absurdities of Trump’s Washington. For Politico, Adam Wren and Eric Bazail-Eimil tell it well.

Another week, another tale of staggering Trump corruption. For ProPublica, Joshua Kaplan, Justin Elliott, and Alex Mierjeski show how an Indian billionaire bought off the administration by pouring cash into an obscure start-up linked to Donald Trump Jr. If such tales are beginning to seem familiar, the corruption they detail does not get any less staggering, nor does the necessity of such reporting lessen even one iota.

Many Democrats and Republicans Agree That Platner Must Go

…but Sebastian Junger, author of The Perfect Storm, War, and other bestsellers, is not so sure. On his Substack, Junger follows up an account of breakfast with the Democratic Maine Senate candidate with a consideration of events prominently including the New York Times hit piece in which a Republican activist who dated Platner sought to cast doubt on his character. Junger isn’t endorsing Platner, but he is distinctly unimpressed with the candidate’s detractors on both sides of the aisle.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘
