item.title: Elon Musk’s $1.77 Trillion SpaceX IPO: What You Absolutely Need to Know
item.description: Friday is IPO day for SpaceX. There’s been no shortage of hype and breaking news around Elon Musk, the world’s richest man, taking SpaceX ( SPCX ) public. Let’s talk about what it means for retirees, investors, and everyone else. Due to SpaceX’s current, sky-high, $1.77 trillion valuation , set to…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Friday is IPO day for SpaceX. There’s been no shortage of hype and breaking news around Elon Musk, the world’s richest man, taking SpaceX (SPCX) public. Let’s talk about what it means for retirees, investors, and everyone else.

Due to SpaceX’s current, sky-high, $1.77 trillion valuation, set to be finalized Thursday, the company’s initial public offering has broad implications for all of us, including those who don’t invest in the markets. This market entry will touch pensions, 401(k)s, and the broader economy in unpredictable ways.

Unlike traditional mega-cap IPOs, SpaceX lost billions in the year before filing to bring the company public. In fiscal year 2025, SpaceX posted a net loss of $4.94 billion, and xAI, which Musk merged with SpaceX in February 2026, lost $6.36 billion that same year.

In terms of the company valuation Musk and his underwriting banks have arrived at, investment research firm Morningstar has shared that it would shave that down by about 56%.

Given that a 2024 Wall Street Journal story ran with the subhead, “Some executives and board members fear the billionaire’s use of drugs – including LSD, cocaine, ecstasy, mushrooms and ketamine – could harm his companies,” what does it mean that this country’s financial systems are turning themselves into propulsion systems to ensure that Musk becomes a trillionaire this Friday?

In terms of direct implications, SPCX’s launch into the market could have unusual downside for retail investors, pensions, and 401(k)s, while presenting unusual upside for SpaceX insiders, and Elon Musk, himself.

So, are you in the rocket or not? And, if you are, is it a one-way ticket? To answer those questions, it’s important to understand why so much about the SpaceX IPO is vastly different from past practice.

On Jan. 14, 2025, just a week before Joe Biden left office, the Securities and Exchange Commission brought charges against Musk.

A few months later, Donald Trump appointed Paul Atkins SEC chair, as the SEC matter was still awaiting its day in court, and as Musk and his wrecking crew at the so-called Department of Government Efficiency were still rooting around the federal government. (The SEC recently settled the case for a paltry $1.5 million.)

Atkins, a commissioner with the SEC from 2002 to 2008, is an advocate for what he refers to as reducing regulatory burdens. At the time of this appointment, Atkins and his wife had a combined net worth of at least $327 million.

In September 2025, the Atkins SEC issued a policy decision that could have serious implications for both the SpaceX offering and the other upcoming AI mega-cap IPOs from OpenAI and Anthropic.

Contrary to the SEC’s long-held refusal to advance IPOs if the company’s governing documents required that shareholders were limited to private arbitration, the Atkins SEC declared its neutrality on such mandatory arbitration provisions.

SpaceX’s corporate governance documents do, indeed, include a mandatory arbitration provision. What this ultimately means is that Musk won’t have to worry about fending off class-action lawsuits from SpaceX investors, as he has with some of his other companies and organizations.

In November, a coalition of more than 60 institutional investors, including pension funds in California, New York, and Illinois, sent a joint letter to the SEC decrying its newly stated policy of neutrality on mandatory arbitration provisions, warning that “this radical departure from the commission’s decades of precedent will destabilize confidence in U.S. markets.”

That same month, the Council of Institutional Investors sent a similar letter. The American Association for Justice updated its website with a segment headed, “SEC Policy Change to Gut Shareholder Rights.”

The SEC hasn’t changed course, leaving retail and certain passive index-fund investors without a heat shield.

Just a week after Reuters broke its May 2026 story about the provisions within SpaceX’s governance documents, institutional investors wrote to Musk and other SpaceX execs, describing the company’s charter as the “most management-favorable governance structure ever brought to U.S. public markets at this scale.”

Their letter noted that, based on stock share allocation and varying stock class types, Musk would retain roughly 79% of total voting power while holding only 42% of the equity, leaving public shareholders with “the majority of economic exposure without a corresponding proportional voice in the governance of the company.”

This arrangement renders Musk, in their words, “unfireable.” Furthermore, as a controlled-company structure under Nasdaq, SpaceX is allowed to skip standard public protections, with no majority-independent board.

Musk is, for all intents and purposes, the SPOF, or single point of failure.

If Musk gets distracted with his other projects, say Neuralink or Tesla, or shuffles valuable corporate assets between his companies, or makes erratic decisions, it could wipe out billions in value overnight.

In addition to eliminating the ability for everyday investors to initiate a class-action lawsuit, SpaceX’s reincorporation in Texas allows it to take advantage of the fact that, as of May 2025, Texas law permits publicly traded corporations to establish strict minimum ownership thresholds of up to 3% for shareholders to file derivative lawsuits.

SpaceX has taken full advantage of this provision. Its bylaws require that a shareholder own billions of dollars in stock, or roughly $52 billion, in order to file a derivative suit, which is the most likely kind of suit to be filed against a controlled company like SpaceX.

The result is that the entire $1.77 trillion estimated valuation is tethered to a single, fairly volatile individual who does not answer to shareholders.

Buckle up. No matter what Musk promises publicly, no matter what that does to the stock price, whether those promises materialize within the represented timeframe, or ever, there is effectively no private legal recourse for investors, thanks to changes by the Atkins SEC.

Action could only come from the SEC or the Justice Department, and this administration has no real appetite for holding Musk accountable.

Why don’t all U.S. indices, which are tracked by investment funds tied to pensions and 401(k)s, wait to add SpaceX until the company has time to, say, post a profit? One index has decided to wait. But several other major benchmark indices went in the other direction.

In a complete break with precedent, Nasdaq-100 and Russell 1000 have changed their rules in order to fast-track investors into many new mega-cap stocks almost from day one. These changes mean funds may be forced to acquire the SpaceX stock at a point when hype around a new stock generates buying activity based largely on excitement versus actual value.

How does that tie in to reporting that pensions and 401(k)s will be affected?

Any index fund, say QQQ for example, that tracks these indices will be unable to refuse to include the stock in their funds. In fact, due to its deal size, SPCX will be so heavily weighted within the indices these funds track that the funds may be required to sell off shares of more stable companies in order to make room to acquire SPCX stock. That inclusion in tracked indices will trigger mandatory, multi-billion-dollar forced buying.

In the past, a company with a very high valuation had to trade publicly for at least a month, and sometimes up to a full year, before major index funds like Nasdaq-100 or Russell 1000 would add the stock. Not so, in the case of SpaceX.

To gauge the enormity of SpaceX’s current $1.77 trillion valuation, it is roughly 7.5 times the size of Alibaba’s 2014 IPO valuation of roughly $236 billion (when adjusted for inflation). Prior to SPCX, Alibaba had the largest deal size of any U.S.-listed IPO. But, unlike SpaceX, Alibaba had been very profitable in the fiscal year prior to its IPO.

Because SPCX will be so heavily weighted within the indices that include it, you might expect these indices to approach its inclusion with an abundance of caution. In fact, the opposite has occurred within several of the major U.S. benchmark indices.

In March, it was reported that Musk was conditioning listing SpaceX stock on the Nasdaq on early inclusion in the Nasdaq-100.

Just three weeks later, Nasdaq announced that it had created a new Fast Entry program that permitted newly public mega-cap (i.e., high-valuation) companies to be added to the Nasdaq-100 index after only 15 days of trading. Previously, its waiting period was at least three months, but could extend to more than a year.

For context, Facebook, now Meta Platforms, had to wait seven months to join the Nasdaq-100 following its May 2012 IPO. Unlike SpaceX, Facebook had demonstrated that it was highly profitable, with a 27% net profit margin in the year before its IPO. In the past, these standard delays were a deliberate decision to allow the stock to season, or cool down, before forcing funds that track it to start acquiring shares.

Instead, in creating its new Fast Entry rule without adding an S&P 500-style profitability requirement, Nasdaq is exposing the Nasdaq-100 to unprofitable, newly public, mega-cap companies during their unstable early introduction to the market.

By the end of May, FTSE Russell had followed suit, altering its rules for the Russell 1000 index by allowing mega-caps to be added to its index after just five trading days, when it had previously required a wait of between one and four months.

In a late-breaking addition, index MSCI (Morgan Stanley Capital International) announced this week that it, too, was fast-tracking SPCX into funds that track its index, to occur just 10 days after SpaceX goes public.

If SpaceX’s stock were a rocket, it could be said that these indices were intentionally introducing Pogo oscillation, forcing index funds into the rocket during its period of near-maximum volatility.

S&P Global had initially considered taking the same route, but cooler heads prevailed. Last week, the S&P announced that it wouldn’t change its requirements to allow for fast entry into its major indices, which blocked SpaceX from S&P 500 inclusion. Instead, SpaceX will be required to trade publicly for 12 months before it becomes eligible.

The fast-tracked inclusion by other indices creates a built-in pool of buyers in the form of everyday retail investors, pension funds, and 401(k) account owners who will involuntarily provide a re-entry capsule for insiders. Theirs is a one-way ticket. Wall Street estimates that passive index funds will buy roughly $30 billion worth of SpaceX stock within just 15 trading days of the IPO.

SpaceX seeks to raise $75 billion at IPO. The retail investor offer period officially closed on Wednesday. Recent reports indicate that demand is approaching four times the available shares.

If demand remains as oversubscribed as indicated, underwriters have up to 30 days post-IPO to exercise a “greenshoe” option. This allows for the introduction of an additional 83.3 million shares, which could inject another $11.24 billion or so into SpaceX’s cash reserves by mid-July, flowing more shares into the market that will, given demand, be picked up immediately.

The passive index funds must buy those shares directly from the existing public float on the open market, which will trigger an unprecedented supply squeeze that could cause the stock price to spike artificially.

When a company IPOs, its first float represents the shares made available to the public.

Though Nasdaq previously required a strict minimum float of at least 10% of the company in order to be included in its Nasdaq-100 index, Nasdaq has altered this rule in SpaceX’s favor, as well. SpaceX will be allowed to keep almost the entire company behind closed doors.

Nasdaq has permitted SpaceX to release only a sliver of its shares for public trading, reported to be around 4.2% of its total shares, which allows Musk to maintain ironclad voting control over the company.

In yet another twist, of the 555.6 million total shares SpaceX plans to sell at a firm price of $135 per share, the 30% retail investor allocation that had previously been reported has, today, been revised down to something closer to 20%. This allocation remains significantly higher than a typical retail allocation, which is closer to 5% to 10%, and is thought to be an effort to capitalize on Musk's fan base.

In addition, Fidelity, whose offerings span investment management, financial planning, brokerage, and fintech services, apparently decided the time was ripe to alter its inviolable rules.

As one of the brokerages that will offer pre-trade allocations of the stock, along with Robinhood, SoFi, E*TRADE, and Schwab, Fidelity reduced its typical $500,000 household asset requirement to get in on an IPO of this size to $2,000, in order to accommodate the unusually high percentage of SpaceX’s IPO shares that are being made available to retail investors.

Fidelity is also penalizing retail investors who flip the stock within the first 15 days of the IPO.

Separately, SpaceX has reserved 5% of the total IPO shares for “certain employees and persons” via a Directed Share Program (DSP) managed by Morgan Stanley. Unusually, these specific shares will not be subject to standard post-IPO lockup restrictions. Per the prospectus, these insiders can sell their stock as early as day one.

At the other end of the spectrum, Musk set terms whereby all of his shares (and those of some large insiders) are locked up for a full year.

But this is not the case for all insiders. SpaceX’s S-1 filing outlines a layered lockup structure that allows equity holders whose pre-IPO shares are locked up to sell off their shares in rolling increments of 7% starting just 70, 90, 105, 120, and 135 days after the IPO, instead of the normal 180 days.

Every time DSP or locked-up insiders’ shares become available for resale, SpaceX’s investable float grows, increasing its weight in the Russell 1000 and Nasdaq-100 and forcing index funds that track those benchmarks to buy more shares, because the funds are market-cap weighted.

Combined with the tiny amount of float, this creates a scenario where there is necessary but artificial demand for the stock based on the company’s relative size within indices.

The net is that Musk has allowed certain groups and individuals to sell their shares early, and if (or when) that happens, the price would likely drop, and investors whose index funds are exposed to SpaceX would absorb that loss.

In other words: Re-entry capsule for me, forced stock acquisition for thee.

SpaceX, per Musk, is plotting to send one million solar-powered satellite data centers to orbit the Earth, as part of his bet on AI.

In February, SpaceX absorbed Twitter (now known as X) and xAI (which includes his chatbot Grok), in an all-stock merger, shifting the company’s business model into a highly speculative AI infrastructure conglomerate, which it documented when it rewrote its corporate identity in its IPO prospectus.

Legally and financially, Grok, Twitter, and all of Musk’s AI assets now live completely under the SpaceX corporate parent umbrella.

The financials inside SpaceX’s SEC filing reveal that it now spends and loses more money on AI infrastructure than it does on rockets.

In its S-1 amendment on June 1, SpaceX listed water scarcity and power-grid strain as core risk factors. The filing admits that the data centers its AI business requires are facing severe physical bottlenecks because of these resource requirements.

Finally, in a weird side note that underscores the pension boards’ concerns about the mercurial nature of the head of a company they’re forced to fold into their funds, Musk’s IPO filing revealed that SpaceX holds 18,712 Bitcoin, which is down around 40% over the past year, as a treasury reserve asset. Normally, companies keep their backup cash in ultra-safe investments like bonds, but not SpaceX.

If Bitcoin tanks, no big deal, right? It’s only a good chunk of the reserve cash held by an unprofitable, AI-centric company that’s being valued at $1.77 trillion – and which will likely affect your 401(k) and/or pension sooner than later.

For his part, Musk is about to become the world’s first trillionaire. Thank you, retail investors, for flying today.

Catherine Menard is a tech CEO who has worked in the DC tech corridor for two decades with specialized expertise in fraud detection and mitigation. Read Catherine’s Substack here.

Editor’s note: This article is based on publicly available information that was current at the time of publication and is not meant to be construed as investment advice.

item.title: Gwyneth Paltrow Deserves Every Bit of the Hate She's Getting Over Her Israel Ad
item.description: “Ohh not again,” Gwyneth Paltrow moaned breathily as she woke up in a penthouse apartment. She wasn’t talking about Israeli airstrikes against displaced Palestinian families in tents or their targeted slaughter of paramedics in Lebanon, no, she was talking about Mondays. Waking up is so hard,…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
“Ohh not again,” Gwyneth Paltrow moaned breathily as she woke up in a penthouse apartment. She wasn’t talking about Israeli airstrikes against displaced Palestinian families in tents or their targeted slaughter of paramedics in Lebanon, no, she was talking about Mondays.

Waking up is so hard, “Gwynicide” – as the internet has since christened her – continued as she sipped from her coffee that “needs a coffee” but running apparently makes you feel better. Running is so good for the spirit, the actress mused in the now viral ad as she runs in Central Park, and so is living in iconic buildings near parks. 51 Park, she coos to a driver. “New York?” he asks, a reasonable question since they are in fact filming in the city. “No,” Paltrow smiles, “Herzliya, Israel.”

We all know many actors are dumb-dumbs, but now it must be said that they are also incomprehensibly evil and greedy. Perhaps the venom from the bee-sting beauty treatments that Paltrow once promoted as a wellness influencer slowly seeped into her brain, but she signed on to be the face of a luxury tower project in Israel.

51Park (so original! Very native, indigenous name!) was built in Herzliya, an Israeli city – named after the founder of Zionism – that was established in 1924 on land that was part of the Palestinian village of al-Haram. The village’s Palestinian population was expelled from the area during the 1948 ethnic cleansing campaign known as the Nakba. And today, encouraged by the Oscar-winning actress, you too can have a piece of blood on your hands for just a few million dollars.

The irrelevance of celebrities has never been more obvious. Remember when actresses wore black on the red carpet to denounce violence and harassment against women? Shocking that that didn’t result in a global drop in femicide. What about when U2 put out an idiotic word salad statement on Gaza that included, “we are not experts on the politics of the region.” Yeah, Bono, we know. What about when the most that some of your favorite Hollywood stars did while Israel maimed and murdered children was wear teeny, tiny, red pins over their couture gowns to call – more like whimper – for a ceasefire.

But even in this universe of cowardice and brainlessness, Gwyneth Paltrow selling apartments in an apartheid state where non-Jews don’t have equal rights to Jews, children are jailed without charge, and rape is practiced against Palestinians held in Israeli prisons as a matter of policy, is truly beyond the pale. Her ad for the luxury apartments ran just days after Israeli soldiers were filmed on camera shooting Sam Abu Haikal, a 7-month-old baby, killing him. This week, Israeli soldiers murdered Jad Suleliman, an 8-year-old boy, who was still wearing his backpack from school when he was killed. On my Instagram feed, I scrolled from Paltrow’s glossy advertisement to a video of Mohammad Al Khatib, a newborn baby whose mother was killed by an Israeli airstrike on his tent. Where Mohammad’s little baby foot should be is a stump wrapped in gauze, yet another victim of Israel’s targeting of children. Gaza now has the largest cohort of child amputees in the entire world. Perhaps someone should tell Paltrow that what Israel is ‘iconic’ for isn’t apartment buildings but hunting journalists, bombing healthcare workers, shooting children in the head, and allegedly raping activists on the Global Sumud Flotilla.

She could claim ignorance, I suppose, because from everything I know about Paltrow, it isn’t clear to me that she knows how to read. On Goop, her dodgy influencing site, she has advised women to steam their vaginas, insert jade eggs into their privates to help their “feminine energy” – practices that medical professionals urgently warned against – and has sold everything from do-it-yourself coffee enemas, $15,000 gold plated vibrators, and sparkly Ouija boards. Paltrow’s vapidity is not comic, but deeply sinister. On her ‘The Goop Podcast,’ she recently hosted the co-founder of Anduril, a drone company aimed at making killing easier and cheaper for the U.S. military, for a fun chat. She has starred in videos with prominent Zionist influencer Noa Tishby, who once served in the Israeli government. And among the stupider things she has sold on Goop was a “sex dungeon leather chaise longue” with a price tag of nearly $30,000 dollars. I’m not sure anyone will remember Paltrow’s Goop guides to yawning (yawn #1 only has five steps!), but it will be impossible to forget her “tacky and evil” support of Israel during its holocaust of Gaza.

Fatima Bhutto is an award-winning author and journalist. ‘Gaza: The Story of a Genocide,’ a collection of essays she co-edited, and her memoir, ‘The Hour of the Wolf,’ are available now.

item.title: The Belfast Riots Are an Anti-Migrant Pogrom - and the British Media Has Serious Questions to Answer

The Belfast Riots Are an Anti-Migrant Pogrom - and the British Media Has Serious Questions to Answer…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
In his first column for Zeteo UK, acclaimed journalist and author Owen Jones looks at how the lie about immigrants pushed by British media and political elites is fueling the violence we see today.

The Belfast Riots Are an Anti-Migrant Pogrom - and the British Media Has Serious Questions to Answer

In his first column for Zeteo UK, acclaimed journalist and author Owen Jones looks at how the lie about immigrants pushed by British media and political elites is fueling the violence we see today.

Men burn debris to create a barricade on a street in Belfast as anti-immigrant violence rocks the city on June 9, 2026. Photo by Charles McQuillan/Getty Images

item.title: 📕 🤪 ‘Not Fun’: Trump Officials Worry This Book Will Send Him Into a Psychotic Rage
item.description: On this day in 1963, Buddhist monk Thich Quang Duc publicly burned himself to death in Saigon, in protest of religious intolerance – creating one of the most famous images of the Vietnam War era. Welcome to another Thursday morning in Donald Trump’s America, Zeteo fans – it’s Swin again 👋. It will…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1963, Buddhist monk Thich Quang Duc publicly burned himself to death in Saigon, in protest of religious intolerance – creating one of the most famous images of the Vietnam War era.

Welcome to another Thursday morning in Donald Trump’s America, Zeteo fans – it’s Swin again 👋. It will never stop being weird to me how much the president of the United States sits in the White House, a place where treaties are signed and war and peace are decided, and devotes a tremendous amount of time to staring at his television, getting egregiously mad. It is also upsetting how much we have to take moments like these seriously, because with President Trump, they can trigger highly consequential actions.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we dive right into one such episode of President Flatscreen TV Addict shouting at the Idiot Box – and what it could mean for the world. Plus, AIPAC enters the ring in Michigan for Haley Stevens, Graham Platner’s Democratic primary win in Maine roils all sides in DC, and Iran and the U.S. trade strikes for a second day.

Our blundering, bloodlusting president woke up on Wednesday and saw the hosts of MS Now’s ‘Morning Joe’ discussing a New York Times excerpt titled ‘Inside the White House Freakout Over the Epstein Files,’ adapted from Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman’s soon-to-be-released book, Regime Change.

Before the episode of ‘Morning Joe’ was over, Donald Trump – glaring at his TV, getting madder and madder – was back on his social media app, ranting about the show and its “laughing stock” host for even talking about the Times article. (Trump did not mention Jeffrey Epstein in his online outburst, but sources familiar with his thinking tell me unequivocally that’s what set him off on Wednesday morning.)

Among the embarrassing revelations in the report, the story includes the word “nipple” six times, in remarkable sentences like: “The vice president said he thought the president would be OK with releasing the nipple-related documents, arguing that Trump had been accused of worse,” and: “One official would later describe it as a ‘surreal’ experience to be discussing nipples in the White House Situation Room.”

Objectively speaking, this is unbelievably funny. The nominal leader of the free world – who can’t seem to stop falling asleep in meetings, pointlessly tanking the economy, and needlessly invading countries for his own amusement – is having his elderly brain consumed with rage about a book filled with humiliating accounts of nipples and warfare in the Trump-Vance era.

It’s the kind of comedy that only Paul Verhoeven could have made, except it’s real. And yet… there are people, including some senior Trump administration officials, who will tell you that you shouldn’t be merely laughing this off.

Here’s why…

“It’s not fun,” one senior Trump appointee tells me. “I like Jonathan and Maggie, and I really don’t want the president to order the… FBI to go raid their homes.”

You can dismiss anonymous Trump advisers all you want, but that isn’t an irrational concern for anyone to have, not in this administration. The president has been furious about this book for months, and that fury hasn’t abated. If anything, it’s escalated and will only continue to do so once the book comes out this month.

Yes, Donald J. Trump is a world-historically catty and temperamental man who screams about a whole host of topics, trivial and not. But multiple U.S. officials and Trump advisers, in and close to the administration, tell me his antipathy right now – toward this forthcoming book, the Times, and the two authors who wrote it – is uniquely severe. Some of these sources, based on their direct knowledge of his sustained, paranoid, and psychosis-level anger and obsession over this, say they wouldn’t be shocked if his visceral reaction to the book caused him to further escalate his domestic war on the media and speech – or even if his wrath flowed over into his decision-making regarding his wars abroad.

“He’s that mad about this,” adds the senior appointee.

I need to stress again that none of this is unreasonable to at least consider. If I were to reach for a historical parallel, I might pick Richard Nixon, and how leak after leak, news article after news article, really did lead to the paranoid, malicious president giving increasingly hysterical, self-destructive orders. But we don’t even need the historical parallel! We know what President Trump has already ordered.

Here at Zeteo, we revealed to you in early April that Trump was so enraged by the first Regime Change excerpt in the Times that he ordered top officials to launch a widespread leak hunt throughout the government, to find and punish the co-authors’ anonymous sources. A month later, we told you how an “ even more paranoid” Trump was pushing Justice Department officials to raid journalists’ houses or even threaten to jail them until they sold out their sources. And this month, I reported on how Trump’s massive leak hunt had largely stalled out… because too many of the senior officials tasked with finding the leakers

As for any concerns that the president’s fury over an unreleased book could perhaps spill over into other realms…

Just minutes after Trump vented yesterday on Truth Social about ‘Morning Joe’ (and, without naming them, Haberman and Swan), the president posted: “Iran is all talk and no action. The Bully of the Middle East is DEAD!!! They’ve taken too long to negotiate a deal that would have been great for them, now they will have to pay the price!!!”

Three U.S. officials tell me that while Trump has swung wildly between begging for “peace” with Iran and ordering the Pentagon to gear up to perpetrate a ton of war crimes in recent weeks, he is once again back to wanting to wage more war and picking new targets to bomb.

“We’ll see how long it takes for him to chicken out again,” one of the officials says.

As of Wednesday night, it wasn’t looking good.

  • Hot war:After a first day of resumed strike and counter-strike, Trump began Wednesday bythreatening“the Bully of the Middle East,” saying Iran would “have to pay the price!!!” for a failure to reach a deal to end the war. Hours later, the two countries were trading strikes for a second day.
  • Damage report:The U.S. military said it targeted military installations and fired on an oil tanker trying to run its blockade. Three Indian sailors aboard the tanker werekilled. Iran said U.S. strikes hit two reservoirs in Sirik, cutting off water to 20,000 people for hours. Targeting civilian infrastructurecould be a war crime. Trump alsothreatenedto bomb bridges and power plants.
  • Iran responds:Tehran said its own strikes targeted Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan. A Revolutionary Guard commanderpromisedto “turn the entire region into hell.”
  • New threat:U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth said “Iran would be wise” to end the war, “otherwise they will have to deal with the types of plans I just had a chance to see.” A spokesperson said Tehran needed “to reassess” its stance on talks, as “any diplomatic process requires a minimum stable environment.”
  • Oil claim:Responding to a question about inflation, Trump claimed the U.S. had been secretly taking “millions of barrels of oil” from Iran “every night,” adding: “And that’s why it’s at $85-90 a barrel instead of $250.” In fact, he was referring to amuch-reported, limited effortto escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Lebanon latest:Israeli strikes in southern Lebanonkilled at least 13yesterday. Thedeath tollfrom Israeli attacks is now at least 3,696, including 730 children, women, andmedics, with around 11,400 injured. Two people were killed in Sidon when a drone struck a car, generatingstartling footageof the fiery aftermath.

AIPAC is spending big in the Michigan Democratic U.S. Senate primary, in which Zeteo/Drop Site News polling found that 64% of likely voters would be less likely to support a candidate who receives donations from AIPAC and other pro-Israel groups.

As reported by the Detroit News, AIPAC “made $2.33 million worth of airtime reservations across Michigan markets on Monday for an advertisement that aims to boost [Rep.] Haley Stevens in the contentious U.S. Senate Democratic primary, marking the group’s first ad spending in the Michigan race this cycle.”

Polling shows a tight race between Stevens and Abdul El Sayed, a progressive backed by Senator Bernie Sanders, with state Senator Mallory McMorrow in third.

The pro-Stevens ad, paid for by United Democracy Project, AIPAC’s super PAC, introduces Stevens as “chief of staff for President Obama’s auto rescue, saving over 200,000 Michigan jobs.” Obama is shown saying, “By the way, she was there. She was a critical part of my team that helped the American auto industry come roaring back.” The ad says that “in Congress, Haley successfully fought to cap the price of insulin at $35 a month. Now she’s fighting back against Trump’s Medicare cuts.”

Stevens, the ad says, is “a proven fighter for Michigan.”

The congresswoman’s campaign website contains text in a “What Voters Need to Know” section. It looks an awful lot like what’s known as a “red box,” or an area that campaigns use on their website to publicly urge allies to push certain messages, because while super PACs and dark money groups can accept donations of any size, they are legally prohibited from directly coordinating with candidates as it relates to advertising.

The AIPAC ad hits some of the same notes on the website. “Haley is standing up to Trump’s attempts to cut Medicare,” the ad says, as it praises her for “capping out-of-pocket prices on insulin at $35 per month.”

Back in April, the Zeteo/Drop Site poll found the three Michigan Democrats near-tied but a third of voters undecided. Since then, amid Israel’s genocidal war in Gaza and brutal invasion of Lebanon, El Sayed, who is strongly critical of Israel, has performed well.

Stevens’s AIPAC links are known, including its support as she won her House seat in 2022. But first-time viewers of the new ad would not necessarily know it was paid for by AIPAC.

  • Maine man:Warning it would be “a fatal mistake to assume [Graham] Platner is too damaged” to win, thanks to reporting about his past, a Republican Senate memocalledfor “total urgency” to beat him. The Democrat’s first general election adpromisedto “take back our government from the Epstein class.”
  • Insults fly:As TrumpcalledPlatner a “low-level thug,”Politicoreportedresistance to backing the Mainer among Democratic senators. One, Fox favorite John Fetterman,calledPlatner “Captain Dick Pic.” Though he has been hit with a sexting scandal, Platner has not been accused of sending intimate photos.
  • Lazy Susan:Asked about Platner’s charge that she lied about protecting abortion rights, given she voted for Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who helped take such rights away, Senator Susan Collinsdeniedit, ignoring a question about whether she regretted that vote as she walked away from a reporter.
  • Recon mission:Trumpcalledfor Republicans to pass $350 billion in military funding by the party-line appropriations process. The GOP chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee – Susan Collins – had already said that won’t happen.
  • Epstein update:The excerpt in theTimesalso included JD Vance’s “extraordinary” suggestion that “the White House enlist Tucker Carlson to interview Epstein’s longtime girlfriend and co-conspirator, Ghislaine Maxwell, in prison.” In Congress, Bill Gatestestifiedin private that hedid not knowof crimes committed by the financier and sex offender.
  • FISA fight:Senate Democratsrejectedan attempt to extend foreign surveillance powers for three weeks, citing opposition to Trump installing MAGA warrior Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence. The powers are set to lapse on Friday.
  • Protest arrests:The FBIsaideight people were arrested at the University of Michigan in Detroit, in relation to pro-Palestinian activism and alleged vandalism.

In New Mexico in 1977, a young Bill Gates had his mugshot taken. What offense had he committed?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Hussam Abu Safiya, a Palestinian doctor from Gaza held without charge by Israel since 2024,appearedby video in the Israeli Supreme Court. It was the first time he had been seen publicly in over a year. He was visibly thinner. His family, lawyers, and former detainees had previouslysaidhe had lost more than 88 lbs, been tortured, and suffered health conditions, including scabies.

  • 🇨🇴

    Meeting nixed:Accordingto theWashington Post, the Trump administration intervened to stop Colombian President Gustavo Petro from meeting New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani. Back in Colombia, a legislative commissionproposedthat Petro be suspended over alleged political meddling in the election to succeed him.
  • 🇬🇧 Belfast plea:After a night of violence over a stabbing allegedly involving a migrant from Sudan, the wounded man’s familyappealedfor calm, saying migrants “make a deeply valuable contribution to our country.” British Prime Minister Keir Starmer led official condemnations, but as darkness fell, further severe disorderoccurred.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:Two babies wereconfirmedto have died in an orphanage in DR Congo, as the outbreak death toll rose to at least 115, with nearly 600 infected.


Donald Trump, being a generous soul, keeps cutting attack ads for Democrats. From the president who gave you “I don’t think about Americans’ financial situation” and “I don’t care about the midterms,” comes… drum roll, please… “I love the inflation.”

Really.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1963, Buddhist monk Thich Quang Duc publicly burned himself to death in Saigon, in protest of religious intolerance – creating one of the most famous images of the Vietnam War era.

Welcome to another Thursday morning in Donald Trump’s America, Zeteo fans – it’s Swin again 👋. It will never stop being weird to me how much the president of the United States sits in the White House, a place where treaties are signed and war and peace are decided, and devotes a tremendous amount of time to staring at his television, getting egregiously mad. It is also upsetting how much we have to take moments like these seriously, because with President Trump, they can trigger highly consequential actions.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we dive right into one such episode of President Flatscreen TV Addict shouting at the Idiot Box – and what it could mean for the world. Plus, AIPAC enters the ring in Michigan for Haley Stevens, Graham Platner’s Democratic primary win in Maine roils all sides in DC, and Iran and the U.S. trade strikes for a second day.

Our blundering, bloodlusting president woke up on Wednesday and saw the hosts of MS Now’s ‘Morning Joe’ discussing a New York Times excerpt titled ‘Inside the White House Freakout Over the Epstein Files,’ adapted from Jonathan Swan and Maggie Haberman’s soon-to-be-released book, Regime Change.

Before the episode of ‘Morning Joe’ was over, Donald Trump – glaring at his TV, getting madder and madder – was back on his social media app, ranting about the show and its “laughing stock” host for even talking about the Times article. (Trump did not mention Jeffrey Epstein in his online outburst, but sources familiar with his thinking tell me unequivocally that’s what set him off on Wednesday morning.)

Among the embarrassing revelations in the report, the story includes the word “nipple” six times, in remarkable sentences like: “The vice president said he thought the president would be OK with releasing the nipple-related documents, arguing that Trump had been accused of worse,” and: “One official would later describe it as a ‘surreal’ experience to be discussing nipples in the White House Situation Room.”

Objectively speaking, this is unbelievably funny. The nominal leader of the free world – who can’t seem to stop falling asleep in meetings, pointlessly tanking the economy, and needlessly invading countries for his own amusement – is having his elderly brain consumed with rage about a book filled with humiliating accounts of nipples and warfare in the Trump-Vance era.

It’s the kind of comedy that only Paul Verhoeven could have made, except it’s real. And yet… there are people, including some senior Trump administration officials, who will tell you that you shouldn’t be merely laughing this off.

Here’s why…

“It’s not fun,” one senior Trump appointee tells me. “I like Jonathan and Maggie, and I really don’t want the president to order the… FBI to go raid their homes.”

You can dismiss anonymous Trump advisers all you want, but that isn’t an irrational concern for anyone to have, not in this administration. The president has been furious about this book for months, and that fury hasn’t abated. If anything, it’s escalated and will only continue to do so once the book comes out this month.

Yes, Donald J. Trump is a world-historically catty and temperamental man who screams about a whole host of topics, trivial and not. But multiple U.S. officials and Trump advisers, in and close to the administration, tell me his antipathy right now – toward this forthcoming book, the Times, and the two authors who wrote it – is uniquely severe. Some of these sources, based on their direct knowledge of his sustained, paranoid, and psychosis-level anger and obsession over this, say they wouldn’t be shocked if his visceral reaction to the book caused him to further escalate his domestic war on the media and speech – or even if his wrath flowed over into his decision-making regarding his wars abroad.

“He’s that mad about this,” adds the senior appointee.

I need to stress again that none of this is unreasonable to at least consider. If I were to reach for a historical parallel, I might pick Richard Nixon, and how leak after leak, news article after news article, really did lead to the paranoid, malicious president giving increasingly hysterical, self-destructive orders. But we don’t even need the historical parallel! We know what President Trump has already ordered.

Here at Zeteo, we revealed to you in early April that Trump was so enraged by the first Regime Change excerpt in the Times that he ordered top officials to launch a widespread leak hunt throughout the government, to find and punish the co-authors’ anonymous sources. A month later, we told you how an “ even more paranoid” Trump was pushing Justice Department officials to raid journalists’ houses or even threaten to jail them until they sold out their sources. And this month, I reported on how Trump’s massive leak hunt had largely stalled out… because too many of the senior officials tasked with finding the leakers

As for any concerns that the president’s fury over an unreleased book could perhaps spill over into other realms…

Just minutes after Trump vented yesterday on Truth Social about ‘Morning Joe’ (and, without naming them, Haberman and Swan), the president posted: “Iran is all talk and no action. The Bully of the Middle East is DEAD!!! They’ve taken too long to negotiate a deal that would have been great for them, now they will have to pay the price!!!”

Three U.S. officials tell me that while Trump has swung wildly between begging for “peace” with Iran and ordering the Pentagon to gear up to perpetrate a ton of war crimes in recent weeks, he is once again back to wanting to wage more war and picking new targets to bomb.

“We’ll see how long it takes for him to chicken out again,” one of the officials says.

As of Wednesday night, it wasn’t looking good.

  • Hot war:After a first day of resumed strike and counter-strike, Trump began Wednesday bythreatening“the Bully of the Middle East,” saying Iran would “have to pay the price!!!” for a failure to reach a deal to end the war. Hours later, the two countries were trading strikes for a second day.
  • Damage report:The U.S. military said it targeted military installations and fired on an oil tanker trying to run its blockade. Three Indian sailors aboard the tanker werekilled. Iran said U.S. strikes hit two reservoirs in Sirik, cutting off water to 20,000 people for hours. Targeting civilian infrastructurecould be a war crime. Trump alsothreatenedto bomb bridges and power plants.
  • Iran responds:Tehran said its own strikes targeted Kuwait, Bahrain, and Jordan. A Revolutionary Guard commanderpromisedto “turn the entire region into hell.”
  • New threat:U.S. Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth said “Iran would be wise” to end the war, “otherwise they will have to deal with the types of plans I just had a chance to see.” A spokesperson said Tehran needed “to reassess” its stance on talks, as “any diplomatic process requires a minimum stable environment.”
  • Oil claim:Responding to a question about inflation, Trump claimed the U.S. had been secretly taking “millions of barrels of oil” from Iran “every night,” adding: “And that’s why it’s at $85-90 a barrel instead of $250.” In fact, he was referring to amuch-reported, limited effortto escort vessels through the Strait of Hormuz.
  • Lebanon latest:Israeli strikes in southern Lebanonkilled at least 13yesterday. She was a critical part of my team that helped the American auto industry come roaring back.” The ad says that “in Congress, Haley successfully fought to cap the price of insulin at $35 a month. Now she’s fighting back against Trump’s Medicare cuts.”

Stevens, the ad says, is “a proven fighter for Michigan.”

The congresswoman’s campaign website contains text in a “What Voters Need to Know” section. It looks an awful lot like what’s known as a “red box,” or an area that campaigns use on their website to publicly urge allies to push certain messages, because while super PACs and dark money groups can accept donations of any size, they are legally prohibited from directly coordinating with candidates as it relates to advertising.

The AIPAC ad hits some of the same notes on the website. “Haley is standing up to Trump’s attempts to cut Medicare,” the ad says, as it praises her for “capping out-of-pocket prices on insulin at $35 per month.”

Back in April, the Zeteo/Drop Site poll found the three Michigan Democrats near-tied but a third of voters undecided. Since then, amid Israel’s genocidal war in Gaza and brutal invasion of Lebanon, El Sayed, who is strongly critical of Israel, has performed well.

Stevens’s AIPAC links are known, including its support as she won her House seat in 2022. But first-time viewers of the new ad would not necessarily know it was paid for by AIPAC.

The Democrat’s first general election adpromisedto “take back our government from the Epstein class.”

One, Fox favorite John Fetterman,calledPlatner “Captain Dick Pic.” Though he has been hit with a sexting scandal, Platner has not been accused of sending intimate photos.
  • Lazy Susan:Asked about Platner’s charge that she lied about protecting abortion rights, given she voted for Supreme Court Justice Brett Kavanaugh, who helped take such rights away, Senator Susan Collinsdeniedit, ignoring a question about whether she regretted that vote as she walked away from a reporter.
  • Recon mission:Trumpcalledfor Republicans to pass $350 billion in military funding by the party-line appropriations process. The GOP chair of the Senate Appropriations Committee – Susan Collins – had already said that won’t happen.
  • Epstein update:The excerpt in theTimesalso included JD Vance’s “extraordinary” suggestion that “the White House enlist Tucker Carlson to interview Epstein’s longtime girlfriend and co-conspirator, Ghislaine Maxwell, in prison.” In Congress, Bill Gatestestifiedin private that hedid not knowof crimes committed by the financier and sex offender.
  • FISA fight:Senate Democratsrejectedan attempt to extend foreign surveillance powers for three weeks, citing opposition to Trump installing MAGA warrior Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence. The powers are set to lapse on Friday.
  • Protest arrests:The FBIsaideight people were arrested at the University of Michigan in Detroit, in relation to pro-Palestinian activism and alleged vandalism.

In New Mexico in 1977, a young Bill Gates had his mugshot taken. What offense had he committed?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:Hussam Abu Safiya, a Palestinian doctor from Gaza held without charge by Israel since 2024,appearedby video in the Israeli Supreme Court. It was the first time he had been seen publicly in over a year. He was visibly thinner. His family, lawyers, and former detainees had previouslysaidhe had lost more than 88 lbs, been tortured, and suffered health conditions, including scabies.

  • 🇨🇴

    Meeting nixed:Accordingto theWashington Post, the Trump administration intervened to stop Colombian President Gustavo Petro from meeting New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani. Back in Colombia, a legislative commissionproposedthat Petro be suspended over alleged political meddling in the election to succeed him.
  • 🇬🇧 Belfast plea:After a night of violence over a stabbing allegedly involving a migrant from Sudan, the wounded man’s familyappealedfor calm, saying migrants “make a deeply valuable contribution to our country.” British Prime Minister Keir Starmer led official condemnations, but as darkness fell, further severe disorderoccurred.

  • 🇨🇩

    Ebola update:Two babies wereconfirmedto have died in an orphanage in DR Congo, as the outbreak death toll rose to at least 115, with nearly 600 infected.


Donald Trump, being a generous soul, keeps cutting attack ads for Democrats. From the president who gave you “I don’t think about Americans’ financial situation” and “I don’t care about the midterms,” comes… drum roll, please… “I love the inflation.”

item.title: '60 Minutes' Correspondent Was Working on Francesca Albanese Profile When Bari Weiss Fired Her
item.description: Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’ Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we bring you a scoop about a story that Cecila Vega was working on for ‘60 Minutes’ when she was…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
Good evening, and welcome to this week’s ‘Ragebait.’

Justin Baragona here, bringing you the latest media dispatches from the right-wing industrial outrage complex and Bari World. In tonight’s edition, we bring you a scoop about a story that Cecila Vega was working on for ‘60 Minutes’ when she was fired. Plus: David Ellison and Co. try to put out Bari Weiss’s fires, who is (or isn’t) watching Stephen Colbert’s replacement, and Fox soothes Trump’s fragile ego after the Madison Square Garden boos.

One of the stories that Cecilia Vega had lined up for the upcoming season of ‘60 Minutes’ when she was unceremoniously fired by Bari Weiss, according to multiple sources familiar with the matter, was a profile on Francesca Albanese – the UN special rapporteur on the Occupied Palestinian Territories.

The story, which had been approved by the show’s then-senior leadership this spring, was still in its early stages at the time of Vega’s termination, and the correspondent had yet to sit down with Albanese for an interview – which was scheduled for the end of this month.

In recent weeks, the segment’s production staff and writers had already conducted background reporting on the UN official and met with Albanese in Rome and Genoa to film b-roll footage for the story. The program was also in the middle of setting up Vega’s shoot with Albanese, only for that to be postponed indefinitely following the “Black Thursday” firings at ‘60 Minutes’ late last month.

“Yes, she was working on a profile of me for ‘60 Minutes,’” Albanese told Zeteo when reached for comment. “In May, her crew traveled to two different locations to follow my work, and we had two additional segments planned: an interview in Tunis, where I live, and another piece before I present my report to the UN General Assembly.”

As of now, according to network sources, the story is on hold since there is no correspondent currently assigned to it following Vega’s dismissal. There are several other stories that were meant for Vega that are also now in limbo.

Albanese has found herself on the receiving end of withering criticism from the Israeli government, the Trump administration, and supporters of Israel for documenting the genocide in Gaza and advocating for legal action against Israel. The U.S. government imposed sanctions on her last year, which a federal judge found violated her First Amendment rights. While she was briefly removed from the sanctions list following that decision, the Trump administration added her back after an appeals court issued an administrative stay on the lower court’s ruling.

I have learned that one of the subjects Vega was interested in exploring with Albanese was how the sanctions have impacted her and her family’s lives, especially that of the UN expert’s eldest daughter, who is an American citizen.

Weiss – an avowed “Zionist fanatic” – has also used her anti-woke, pro-Israel site The Free Press to take aim at the official.

In an editorial last fall, The Free Press’s editors dismissed a scholar association’s resolution accusing Israel of genocide because one of its sources was Albanese, who they claimed was “discredited,” saying it was “another reason not to trust the ‘experts.’”

So it’s fair then to raise an obvious question – did this upcoming story about a loud and vocal critic of Israel’s treatment of Palestinians play a part in Vega’s dismissal?

Vega and representatives for CBS did not respond to requests for comment.

Vega said in a memo after her termination that she and her producing teams had “experienced efforts to insert political bias into our stories.” She added that the show’s reporters had “held back on submitting story pitches about important news topics out of fear of the internal repercussions.”

Amid the “shitshow” engulfing ‘60 Minutes’ over the mass firing of the show’s senior management and several correspondents, Vega’s termination has been the one that has left many scratching their heads. Not only was she the youngest of the show’s seasoned reporters, having just joined in 2023 from ABC News, but her contract wasn’t due to expire until March 2027.

Yet, while Sharyn Alfonsi had long been expected to be let go due to her public run-ins with Weiss over the “Inside CECOT” story, and Scott Pelley was fired following his heated confrontation with Weiss’s handpicked new ‘60 Minutes’ boss Nick Bilton, a target may have been placed on Vega’s back even before Weiss was hired.

Months before David Ellison closed his merger with Paramount and eventually installed Weiss as CBS News’s chief editor, a ‘60 Minutes’ story by Vega over the way the Biden administration was handling the war in Gaza sparked loud complaints from not only the Anti-Defamation League and other pro-Israel groups, but also Shari Redstone – the company’s then-owner and a passionate supporter of Israeli causes.

That January 2025 story, which centered on ex-State officials resigning in protest over the U.S. policy in Gaza, prompted Redstone to demand immediate action – eventually leading to the resignations of top CBS News leaders over concerns of corporate interference. This came as ‘60 Minutes’ was already under intense pressure due to Trump’s meritless lawsuit. (We know how that ended.)

With the Ellisons and Weiss also being extremely fervent supporters of Israel, network staffers and insiders I’ve spoken to have openly wondered if that Gaza war story was the impetus behind Vega’s firing.

Since arriving at the network last fall, many of Weiss’s personnel and programming decisions have at least appeared to be based on pro-Israel sentiment. Whether it’s making Tony Dokoupil the face of ‘CBS Evening News,’ hiring an Israeli-flag-waving correspondent, firing a well-respected bureau chief for supposedly running her office like a “Hamas cell,” laying off the network’s leading Gaza reporter after Mike Huckabee complained about her, or allowing Benjamin Netanyahu to choose which reporter interviews him, critics have ample evidence to accuse her of pushing an Israel First agenda at CBS.

At the same time, there could be another reason Weiss targeted Vega, at least according to one senior CBS News staffer.

“If you look at The Free Press, or if you look at any of Bari’s own thoughts on it, she would just look at Cecilia Vega as some DEI hire and not as an extraordinarily talented journalist who rose through the ranks at ABC News and hit the highest heights of America’s number one legacy news network and who is so good, she was recognized by ‘60 Minutes,’ which is the number one news broadcast show,” the staffer said.

“Cecilia is extraordinarily gifted, talented, and qualified, and so I just feel like, as well as whatever her perception of her potentially being pro-Palestinian, or whatever it is, I also think the bigger issue is that Bari just doesn’t think women of color deserve to be in those spaces,” the staffer added.

While we may never fully know why Vega was let go, we will keep an eye on ‘60 Minutes’ this upcoming season to see if the Albanese profile makes it to air.

Days after Bilton vowed to protect the show from corporate interference in a conciliatory memo, prompting the program’s last remaining correspondents to stick around, Lesley Stahl revealed to ‘60 Minutes’ staffers – during a champagne toast meant to boost morale – that Ellison promised her he’d respect the editorial independence of the iconic newsmagazine while apologizing for all the recent turmoil.

Of course, folks at CBS News have heard this before from Ellison. He made that exact promise in August after he fully took over Paramount, only to immediately install a pro-Trump ombudsman and foist Weiss on the network, leading the staff to quickly lose trust in him.

Still, whether he’s being genuine or not, it’s apparent that Ellison realizes just how much of a fire-alarm fire Weiss started with her haphazard overhaul of ‘60 Minutes,’ which has only led to wall-to-wall negative headlines – like Pelley telling the New York Times that Weiss is incompetent and putting her thumb on the scale for Trump. Or Alfonsi and Vega

According to Status’s Oliver Darcy, Weiss has been holed up in a secure bunker within CBS headquarters while Ellison and other Paramount bigwigs look to clean up the mess she created. All the while, rumors continue to swirl that, with the Warner Bros. deal fast approaching, Paramount brass is looking to find a veteran media executive to share the duties of running a combined CNN-CBS News with Weiss.

However, the rub – at least based on the media spin coming out of Paramount – is that Ellison wants Weiss to maintain editorial and creative control while having another person handle the business-side operations. What seasoned executive in their right mind would cede editorial duties to someone who has done nothing but spark dumpster fires these past eight months?

‘Comics Unleashed,’ the low-budget comic talk show that replaced Stephen Colbert’s ‘The Late Show’ on CBS’s late-night schedule, is actively repelling viewers after just two weeks in its new time slot.

According to Nielsen data, the program – which has been around in syndication for over 20 years – averaged just 764,000 total viewers and 114,000 in the key 25-54 advertising demographic last week. This is down from the previous week, which saw ‘Comics Unleashed’ draw a nightly average of 849,000 viewers.

Comparatively, ‘The Late Show’ averaged 2.7 million viewers a night during its final season, and pulled in a massive audience of 6.7 million for its finale – which came months after Paramount canceled the long-running franchise.

While the company said it axed the MAGA-skewering Colbert’s show “purely for financial reasons” and alleged the top-rated program lost $40 million annually, Ellison’s merger with Paramount was approved by the Trump administration just days after the cancellation was announced.

Byron Allen, the host and producer of ‘Comics Unleashed,’ appears to be unfazed by the show’s terrible ratings. The stand-up comic turned mogul, whose media company owns the program, is paying Paramount $15 million to run the show during Colbert’s airtime in exchange for advertising revenue.

“CBS has won big-time because they have zero production costs and now they are saving $55 million a year,” he told the Los Angeles Times this week.

Leave it to Trump’s favorite morning show to obediently tell its Dear Leader that the Knicks fans were actually shouting “Boo-urns” when they showered the president with jeers during his Madison Square Garden appearance at Game 3 of the NBA Finals.

After Fox’s social media channels told its followers the crowd chanted “USA!” with “President Trump in attendance,” and its digital outlet merely reported that the arena reacted “to Trump far differently than college football fans,” ‘Fox & Friends’ got right to work on Tuesday morning.

With a chyron blaring “Trump Gets Mixed Reception From Fans,” Brian Kilmeade – a member of Trump’s ‘Fox News Cabinet’ – insisted that the president “did get a good response” while comparing the crowd’s reaction to a Mike Tyson fight.

“I would say it was mixed,” Kilmeade proclaimed to viewers – and one in particular. “There were people cheering. I’ll challenge anybody on that – I was sitting right there.”

As if the North Korea vibes weren’t already on the nose, ‘Fox & Friends’ even did a segment on how the “aggressive rhetoric” coming from Kim Jong Un has quieted down, thanks to Donald Trump – only to then jump directly into a discussion about the Knicks game.

Watch out for my newsletter, ‘Ragebait,’ every week. I focus on the right-wing outrage-industrial complex and the intersection of our political and media landscapes. Send any tips on Signal (justinbaragona.85).

Check out more from Zeteo:

item.title: 'He Didn’t Carry a Weapon': Israel Is Killing Lebanon's Medics at an Unprecedented Rate
item.description: BEIRUT, Lebanon – Zeinab Qassir had been preparing for her brother’s wedding. Hussein, 37, was weeks away from getting married when, on the morning of May 22, he woke to the sound of an explosion near his home in the southern Lebanese village of Deir Qanoun. A Syrian family had been struck while on…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
BEIRUT, Lebanon – Zeinab Qassir had been preparing for her brother’s wedding.

Hussein, 37, was weeks away from getting married when, on the morning of May 22, he woke to the sound of an explosion near his home in the southern Lebanese village of Deir Qanoun. A Syrian family had been struck while on a motorbike. He learned there were injured people. He dressed in his paramedic gear and moved toward the site.

Zeinab tried to stop him. She had seen a drone circling low over the area.

He went anyway.

“I held him before he left,” she tells me, “without knowing it was the last time I would see him alive.”

A short time after paramedics arrived at the site, a woman filming from her window captured the moment a second strike hit their ambulance.

Zeinab ran toward the scene.

“When I arrived, Hussein was still alive,” she says. “There were victims on the ground around me. I didn’t see any of them. All my attention was on my brother as he took his final breaths.”

“Hussein loved ambulance work since he was a child. He didn’t carry a weapon,” says Zeinab. “He woke up from sleep because he heard there were civilians who needed help, and he went to save them.”

Israel’s targeting of medical personnel in Lebanon is not new. But the scale is.

Since March 2, when Israel re-invaded southern Lebanon, near-daily attacks on first responders have left at least 131 paramedics killed and more than 385 wounded. At least 36 centers have been hit, and 169 vehicles have been damaged, according to Lebanon’s Health Ministry.

Their work, especially in the southern part of the country, is increasingly important, as Israel continues to ramp up attacks. Since the latest escalation began, Israeli strikes have killed more than 3,665 people and wounded at least 11,321, with over a million displaced from the south, where Israel has burned entire villages to set up a so-called buffer zone.

Among the dead healthcare workers, about one in five were killed after the supposed “ceasefire” took effect in April, according to World Health Organization figures.

Joud Suleiman was 15 years old.

He had been coming to the Nabatiyeh ambulance center since he was 5 or 6, trailing the paramedics and helping them carry food parcels and bread to families in the area. By the time the latest hostilities broke out, he had been embedded in the rhythms of the team for most of his life.

His father, Mohammed Suleiman, leads the southern city of Nabatiyeh’s ambulance unit. When strikes intensified, he tried more than once to send his son to Beirut. Joud refused each time.

“He said, ‘I’ll come back by taxi if you throw me out of Nabatiyeh’,” Mohammed recalls. “This is my decision, and I’m staying to help the paramedics with you.”

Because of his age, Joud wasn’t deployed as a field medic. He handled logistics: preparing food supplies, readying equipment, running support for the teams in the field. On the morning of March 24, he and colleague Ali Jaber set out on a motorcycle marked with ambulance insignia and emergency lights and headed to a community kitchen called “Beit al-Zaman al-Jamil” – or the “House of the Beautiful Old Days” in English – to prepare meals for crews working the area.

An ambulance vehicle was roughly 200 meters (650 feet) ahead of them. Then a drone appeared over the same road.

“It never crossed my mind that Ali and Joud could be the targets,” Mohammed says. “They were known to everyone as paramedics. Everything they were wearing, everything with them, confirmed their humanitarian identity”.

He called Joud’s phone. No answer. Others tried Ali. Nothing.

When Mohammed reached the site, the motorcycle was burning. Both young men were dead. Ali was in his blue ambulance uniform. Joud was wearing his jacket and helmet, the ambulance insignia visible on both.

“It was the first strike we faced on a Nabatiyeh ambulance – the first time we confronted the targeting of paramedics this way,” Mohammed says. “The shock was severe. They lost two of their closest friends.”

The funerals were held. Then the team went back to work.

“We buried our martyrs and returned to the field,” Mohammed says. “What happened will not push us to retreat. It will make us hold tighter to the mission they died for.”

The attacks have not been limited to moving vehicles. Mohammed Shakroun, the civil defense training official at the Islamic Risala Scout Association, a prominent Lebanese youth and humanitarian organization, described ambulances hit while responding to prior strikes, crews targeted while evacuating the wounded, stationary vehicles destroyed at their centers, and the centers themselves reduced to rubble.

There have been “more than 70 direct targeting incidents involving paramedics, centers, and ambulances in the south and Jabal Amel,” Shakroun tells me, “and between 50 and 60 indirect incidents – strikes near work positions, or grenades dropped from drones on crews mid-mission.”

In Nabatiyeh, the Risala Association had around 150 paramedics at the start of the latest hostilities, but only 20 remain – some have been killed, while others have been wounded or displaced and unable to work. At the same time, the group there is operating with only five ambulances after Israeli attacks put roughly 30 out of commission.

Nationwide, the Risala Association began with approximately 1,500 trained emergency and civil defense personnel. The numbers operating in the most exposed zones are now a fraction of that.

Falah Faqih, who heads the Civil Defense center in Nabatiyeh, says 12 of his personnel have been killed and 42 wounded. Nine centers in the region have been completely destroyed.

He spoke with difficulty about what it means to keep working.

“The duty of the call remains the most important,” he tells me, describing the staff and volunteers he lost as his children – including his sister’s son. “They are all my children. They are all one soul.”

The Lebanese Red Cross has lost two volunteers in direct strikes. Youssef Assaf died on March 9 from wounds sustained during a rescue operation in Majdal Zoun. He had four children, the youngest just a few months old. Hassan Badawi was killed on April 12 when a drone struck him as he stepped out of an ambulance with a stretcher during a rescue mission in Bint Jbeil, leaving, according to his colleagues, 300 pieces of shrapnel in his body. He had a pregnant wife and a son at home.

In both cases, the Red Cross said it had shared the coordinates and details of the missions with all parties to the conflict through standard deconfliction channels, including coordination with the UN peacekeeping mission in Lebanon (UNIFIL). The ambulances were fully marked with the Red Cross emblem, lit, and visible from all sides.

“This is why the Red Cross volunteers hug each other and say goodbye before every mission,” says Ali Saad, a liaison coordinator with the Lebanese Red Cross.

The day after Badawi was killed, an Israeli airstrike hit the Red Cross center in Tyre while a wounded person was being transferred, killing him and damaging several ambulances.

The Israeli military has not directly responded to the growing evidence that it targets medical personnel, which is considered a war crime. In mid-March, Israeli military spokesperson Avichay Adraee issued a statement accusing Hezbollah of using medical facilities and ambulances for military purposes. No evidence was provided, and the Lebanese Ministry of Health denied the claim.

Ramzi Kaiss, a Human Rights Watch researcher for Lebanon, says the organization has investigated multiple cases and found no indication that the medical vehicles or facilities struck were being used for military purposes.

The attacks have included “stationary ambulances, fixed ambulance centers, and the homes of paramedics,” Kaiss explains. And they’ve hit a range of relief organizations, meaning the attacks can’t be explained by affiliation with any single party, Kaiss adds. “This weakens the Israeli justifications considerably.”

Ali Sweidan, a legal analyst at The Legal Agenda, notes that the “warnings” Israel sometimes provides do not meet the standards required by international humanitarian law before a protected facility can be struck. General statements issued to medical crews, he argues, cannot replace specific, direct warnings with adequate time to respond.

“The goal appears to go beyond targeting a vehicle or a specific mission,” Sweidan says. “It extends to paralyzing the entire rescue and relief infrastructure in the south.”

Doctors Without Borders, which has been working alongside civil defense teams across Nabatiyeh, warned that the fear of repeated strikes – known as double-tap strikes – has paralyzed the rescue response itself. The response is delayed, or ambulance crews spend only minutes at blast sites. Rescues are delayed. Excavation equipment goes unused. People have been left under rubble for hours – sometimes days.

“We have treated patients whose conditions were critically worsened by these delays,” the organization noted, “including severe trauma cases who later died from their injuries.”

Despite the known risks, first responders continue to put their communities first. It is why Hussein Qassir ignored his sister’s pleas not to go out on that fateful day. It is also why Mohammed Suleiman keeps going out – because someone has to. For these men, it’s duty against everything else, and duty wins, every time, even when everything else includes the people they love most.

The same day Mohammed Suleiman’s son and colleague were buried, his team was back out distributing food aid.

“The most painful thing,” says Suleiman, “is not only losing my son, but that Joud was a child who chose to spend his days serving people and helping those in need.”

Ali Awada is a Lebanese journalist who has covered Lebanese affairs for more than a decade for regional and international outlets.

This story has been produced in collaboration with Egab.

item.title: Is There a Double Standard for World Cup Criticism?
item.description: How beautiful will the beautiful game be this summer, when the World Cup has already been mired by exorbitant ticket prices, a criminal complaint against FIFA’s new president, and of course, entry and visa chaos caused by U.S. President Donald Trump’s vicious crackdown on Middle East and African…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
How beautiful will the beautiful game be this summer, when the World Cup has already been mired by exorbitant ticket prices, a criminal complaint against FIFA’s new president, and of course, entry and visa chaos caused by U.S. President Donald Trump’s vicious crackdown on Middle East and African countries? And are there double standards in the way Western media reacted to recent world cups versus this one?

In this new three-part, deep-dive video series, Zeteo’s editor-in-chief and lifelong Liverpool and England football fan Mehdi Hasan sets out to answer those questions.

Mehdi goes back in history to explain how some of the world’s most shameful leaders have used the World Cup and FIFA’s corrupt behavior to raise their own status on the world stage and also distract from their country’s heinous human rights violations – a practice known as sportswashing.

In this first episode, Mehdi starts off with an authoritarian whose sportswashing is not getting nearly enough attention right now: U.S. President Donald Trump – one of the co-hosts of this summer’s World Cup, despite his administration having threatened to bring rogue ICE agents to the World Cup stadiums.

Throughout this series, Mehdi is also joined by a team of expert guests, like former football player turned political scientist Jules Boycoff, Human Rights Watch Global Initiatives Director Minky Worden, and New Yorker Global Affairs Columnist Ishaan Tharoor.

Trust us when we say, you do NOT want to miss out on this new series from Zeteo!

Paid subscribers can watch this full episode, as well as the two others that will be released soon. Free subscribers can watch a 3-minute preview. Upgrade your subscription today to access this episode, paywall-free, as well as the other two episodes still to come!

Check out Zeteo’s other recent stories:
