item.title: Blood on the Basketball: The NBA Must End Its Deal With the UAE as the Sudan Genocide Worsens
item.description: As the National Basketball Association (NBA) finals get underway, the league faces several vexing problems, including how to stop teams from tanking and players from betting. These issues pale in comparison, however, to the commercial branding deal the league has forged with a government – the…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
As the National Basketball Association (NBA) finals get underway, the league faces several vexing problems, including how to stop teams from tanking and players from betting. These issues pale in comparison, however, to the commercial branding deal the league has forged with a government – the United Arab Emirates (UAE) – accused of supporting a Sudanese militia committing some of the 21st century’s most heinous race- and ethnicity-based human rights crimes.

Ironically, many NBA players have been civil and human rights leaders for decades. During the Jim Crow era in the US, basketball players like Bill Russell, Oscar Robertson, and Kareem Abdul-Jabbar stood up against the gross injustices of that time and advocated for civil rights. More recently, NBA and WNBA players, including Maya Moore, LeBron James, Carmelo Anthony, and Jaylen Brown, advocated for racial justice in America. Others have spoken out about the brutal tactics of ICE agents.

The NBA and WNBA are now one degree of separation from one of the worst cases of racial injustice in the world due to the largest branding deal ever signed with a government by the two leagues. The multi-year deal, announced in 2024, will reportedly generate an estimated $500 million in ad revenue around the following components: the mid-season basketball tournament named the Emirates NBA Cup, the Emirates Air logo on a patch on jerseys of all NBA and WNBA referees, and virtual Emirates branding projected onto the court during every nationally televised NBA game. The New York Knicks, which will play in the finals, have their own $30 million deal, resulting in Experience Abu Dhabi patches on the team jerseys and warm-up jackets.

With their history of human rights advocacy, the dark side of this partnership is one that most players probably don’t know about. While the UAE consistently issues blanket denials of allegations that it supports the militia known as the Rapid Support Forces (RSF), there is ample evidence from human rights groups, news organizations, and others to the contrary.

It’s unlikely that Steph Curry knows that the UAE provides arms and other support to a militia in Sudan that the United Nations says is committing genocide. Genocide is a very specific crime. It is invoked only when acts are committed with the intent to destroy, in whole or in part, a group based on their nationality, ethnicity, race, or religion. In the Sudanese case, it is racial and ethnic identity.

Angel Reese is probably not aware that, largely because of UAE support, the war in Sudan has displaced nearly 14 million Sudanese people, the largest number in the world. That means 14 million people are homeless in part because of the drones, artillery, mortars, and missiles that the UAE reportedly provides to its Sudanese militia partner, which attacks targets often based on their racial and ethnic backgrounds.

LeBron James would probably not have heard that in October 2025, as the NBA Emirates Cup games were underway, the RSF, supported by the UAE, overran the Sudanese city of El Fasher and killed somewhere between 60,000 and 100,000 Sudanese civilians, including burning people alive, again on the basis of their racial or ethnic identity. And that it didn’t just happen overnight; the militia had laid siege to the city for 500 days, attempting to starve the residents, while the world looked on.

Caitlin Clark surely couldn’t have known that the United Nations says that sexual violence in Sudan is “pervasive” and “systematic,” and that the war there is the “world’s worst humanitarian crisis for women and girls.”

Jaylen Brown is unlikely to have come across the fact that children are extensively recruited and used as soldiers in Sudan’s civil war, and that the primary perpetrator of child soldier recruitment is the militia supported by the UAE.

NBA and WNBA players might be disincentivized to speak out about the UAE’s role in these atrocities as they could be fined for conduct detrimental to the league’s commercial interests.

But this is genocide, the world’s most heinous crime, one rooted in racial domination and extermination. Players could at least raise awareness of the plight of the Sudanese people. Some players might even be willing to incur a financial penalty by calling on the UAE to stop supporting the genocidal militia in Sudan.

Meanwhile, sports fans and others concerned with this outrage can raise their voices and call on the NBA, WNBA, and New York Knicks to end their brand relationships with the UAE, and demand that the UAE end its military support for that genocidal militia in Sudan, a link that is deeply antithetical to everything the players and leagues stand for. Money not tainted with so much blood can come from other sponsors.

Millions of Sudanese lives are at stake, and silence simply encourages those responsible.

Omer Ismail is the former Foreign Minister of Sudan during the last civilian-led government before the military coup. John Prendergast is co-founder of The Sentry, which launched the Blood on the Ball campaign

item.title: Will This Doctor Who Went to Gaza Win a Seat in Congress?
item.description: Two years ago, Dr. Adam Hamawy was trapped inside Gaza. Today, he’s on the brink of joining the same legislative body that helped Israel bomb the patients he was treating. Hamawy is running in New Jersey’s 12th congressional district, in the Democratic primary. He is a U.S. Army combat surgeon…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
Two years ago, Dr. Adam Hamawy was trapped inside Gaza. Today, he’s on the brink of joining the same legislative body that helped Israel bomb the patients he was treating.

Hamawy is running in New Jersey’s 12th congressional district, in the Democratic primary. He is a U.S. Army combat surgeon veteran – credited with saving Senator Tammy Duckworth’s life during the Iraq War – who helped treat patients after 9/11, and volunteered to treat patients in Gaza amid Israel’s genocide.

He’s now being accused of having ties to terrorism, with critics attempting to link Hamawy to a cleric who had been convicted in 1995 of seditious conspiracy. Hamawy slams the claims as “ridiculous,” arguing his critics ignore his years of service and have nothing else to talk about. “They’re afraid about what I stand for – and when I go to Congress, the fact that I am fighting a genocide that’s happening right now, and real terrorism, and a terrorist state,” Hamawy told Zeteo’s Prem Thakker. “They’re afraid of that, and would rather go back 30 years and make up a story.”

One of his critics, New Jersey Democratic Congressman Josh Gottheimer, said he has “serious questions and deep concerns” about Hamawy’s supposed “associations with terrorist organizations.”

Hamawy did not mince words in response. “What would you expect from an AIPAC bot, who really has been the most racist, anti-Muslim member of the New Jersey delegation?” he said. “I’m glad that I will hopefully get elected and be in office and be able to stand up to this bully.”

Watch the video above to hear Hamawy’s full conversation with Prem, in which he explains whether he’d support Hakeem Jeffries as party leader, talks about what radicalized him on healthcare in the US, and recounts his experiences treating patients from Ground Zero to Gaza.

We have removed the paywall on this interview. Please subscribe to Zeteo to help us do interviews like this, and so much more.

item.title: 🤫 ‘Reservoir Dogs’: Trump’s Massive Leak Hunt Stalled Out For This Hilarious Reason
item.description: On this day in 1954, Wisconsin Republican Senator Joseph McCarthy claimed communists had infiltrated the CIA and the atomic weapons industry. President Dwight D. Eisenhower dismissed the claim, setting in motion the fall and disgrace of the man behind the “Red Scare.” Happy Tuesday, Zeteo fans –…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1954, Wisconsin Republican Senator Joseph McCarthy claimed communists had infiltrated the CIA and the atomic weapons industry. President Dwight D. Eisenhower dismissed the claim, setting in motion the fall and disgrace of the man behind the “Red Scare.”

Happy Tuesday, Zeteo fans – it’s Swin again. I’ve reported on how Donald Trump’s second administration can be understood primarily as a “den of snitches, ‘freaks,’ and paranoid tyrants.” Since last year, the snitching, the freakishness, and the paranoia and tyranny have only gotten worse – and Trump’s disastrously unpopular bloodbath in the Middle East made matters much worse for Team Trump, turbo-charging the amount of leaking and ass-covering amongst the den of freaks.

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ we have another update for you on how all this backbiting and fascist-‘Office Space’-style governance can take some very humorous twists and turns, at the expense of Trump himself. Also, Trump drops his “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund – for now – and a few Democrats belatedly discover a fraction of spine after marching with Israeli extremists.

In April, Donald Trump was so furious about leaks of highly sensitive information to two journalists that the U.S. president – as Zeteo first reported – ordered senior officials to launch a massive leak hunt throughout his government.

Trump wanted to know who had blabbed to New York Times reporters Maggie Haberman and Jonathan Swan for their forthcoming book Regime Change, including for their fly-on-the-wall

By May, the president was so livid and paranoid about the leaks that he pushed his Justice Department to seriously consider raiding the homes of reporters, or just jailing them until they sold out their anonymous sources.

It’s June now. Given that Trump officials had said this leak hunt was such a “ big deal” to the president, we wanted to check in with our well-placed sources about how the hunt was going, as it stretches on into the summer.

In the past several weeks, it appears to have stalled out. President Trump didn’t get what he wanted, which was to very quickly see some (figurative) heads on plates: to identify which of his trusted appointees had leaked, and find out how he could punish them for, in Trump’s repeated phrasing, such “treason.”

There’s one big reason why the Trump-Vance administration’s sprawling leak hunt into who sang for Swan and Haberman got hobbled. Here’s what we found:

According to an array of sources working in or close to the Trump White House, way, way, way too many Cabinet-level appointees and senior administration officials leaked to the Times reporters to make a leak hunt truly effective.

“We started making a list of likely suspects, and it got too long,” one Trump administration official tells me.

According to sources with direct knowledge of the situation, too many of the people Trump had asked about finding the leakers had themselves, well, leaked to the Regime Change co-authors.

Trump’s leak probes are no joke: They’re part of a much broader pattern of waging blatantly authoritarian warfare against free speech, the free press, whistleblowers, and liberal dissent in this country.

And yet I cannot possibly pretend that it isn’t hysterically funny how Trump has put himself in a position where it’s very hard for him to find the leakers because the senior personnel he tapped to be the leak-hunters-in-chief are also the ones who’ve been doing the leaking. Good help is hard to find!

When asked bluntly by Zeteo if they snitched, two senior officials, one current and one former, said, of course, they talked to Haberman and Swan for the book. Each said they were comfortable anonymously admitting it because that admission, by their own estimations, could have come from basically anybody at their level.

“It’s one of the worst-kept secrets at the White House,” the current official says, adding: “I’m not ratting myself out; that would be ridiculous.”

Another adviser to the president – who refused to say whether they had or hadn’t leaked to Haberman and Swan – compared the Trump-ordered leak hunt to the scene at the end of the Quentin Tarantino movie ‘Reservoir Dogs,’ in which the different characters have loaded guns pointed at one another, ensuring mutual destruction. (Your ‘First Draft’ author, personally, would analogize the situation to the 2007 film ‘Breach,’ when the Laura Linney character reveals that when the FBI wanted to find out who at the bureau was spying for the Soviets, it tasked agent Robert Hanssen with the mole hunt.)

The president, for his part, “has his suspicions” about who leaked certain details, the Trump adviser tells me. This person declined to elaborate.

Naturally, Zeteo can’t ethically out our own sources, who did not, even under the cloak of anonymity, want to provide reportable information that named names. However, you – as a shrewd observer of American politics – can feel confident that if you think of a name of a prominent Team Trump personality, you’re thinking of somebody who blabbed to the Times duo.

In response to a comment request for this story, Trump White House spokesman Davis Ingle would only reply: “What the hell is a Zeteo?” (The White House knows who we are; they have repeatedly called us out by name in public statements, blasted Mehdi as our “nutjob founder,” and dubbed me a “deranged hack” in the recent past. But thanks for the comment, guys! 😀)

Regime Change is due out late this month. Trump is incredibly pissed off about it. Expect his fury to billow up even more as we get closer to the publication date. And to repeat a disclosure I’ve made here at Zeteo before: Swan was one of my groomsmen, and I love him like a brother. So, Mr. Trump, if you’re listening: If you put him in the gulag for his “treason,” just remember that I’ve never met Jonathan, I know nothing of this “Swan” fellow, and I have no idea what you’re talking about… Thanks.

  • Talks chaos:Trump said Iran “really wants to make a deal.” But then the semi-official Tasnim news agencysaidIran was considering closing the Strait of Hormuz and other aggressive measures, while Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, referring to Israeli strikes in Lebanon,said: “Violation on one front is a violation of the ceasefire on all fronts. The U.S. and Israel are responsible for the consequences of any violation.”
  • Lebanon latest:Later, Trumpsaidhe had secured agreement from Benjamin Netanyahu and Hezbollah “that all shooting will stop” in Lebanon, with Israel backing off from attacking Beirut. Lebanonconfirmeda partial ceasefire, but Netanyahu vowed that strikes would continue in the south, where at least eight people have been killed in Israeli attacks since yesterday, including an attack on a hospital. The death tollclimbedto at least 3,433 people.
  • Expletives undeleted:According toAxios, Trump’s call with Netanyahu was not full of diplomatic niceties. A U.S. official said Trump told the Israeli PM: “You’re fucking crazy. You’d be in prison if it weren’t for me. I’m saving your ass. Everybody hates you now. Everybody hates Israel because of this.” Trump then posted that talks to end the war were continuing at “a rapid pace.”
  • ‘Couldn’t care less’:Trump alsotoldCNBC talks with Iran were “very boring” and said: “I really don’t care. I couldn’t care less. If they’re over, they’re over.” The White House put out a postsayingpeople should just “TRUST IN TRUMP.”
  • Prices wrong:Unsurprisingly, amid so much uncertainty and erratic behavior, oil pricessurgedyet again.

Trump’s hugely controversial “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund is not dead yet – but the White House has put it on hold.

First, Axios scooped that the administration “planned to drop” the proposed $1.776 billion fund, announced last month by acting Attorney General Todd Blanche and the cause of immediate outrage over the prospect of payouts to Trump supporters supposedly victimized by law enforcement, including election deniers and rioters who attacked the U.S. Capitol on January 6.

Trump was also given broad immunity from investigation by the Internal Revenue Service, the subject of his spurious lawsuit, which was “settled” to create the fund.

Democrats and Republicans alike were outraged by such naked corruption. But on Monday, tellingly, one source told Axios such backlash merely meant the fund was “dead for now.” Nothing, the outlet noted, “is certain in the Trump administration until it’s officially announced or the president says it himself.”

Later, the Department of Justice released a statement attacking a Virginia judge’s ruling last week that paused the fund until mid-June, but said it would respect the order. The statement also claimed the fund, meant to be governed by DOJ and Trump appointees, would have been “open to anybody who was so weaponized, targeted, or persecuted, whether they were Democrat, Republican, Conservative, Independent, or otherwise.”

To reach for a technical term: Yeah, right.

Notably, not even Trump’s own party is convinced he will not try to revive the fund. Senator John Curtis of Utah told the  New York Times he had “a lot of unanswered questions” and wanted “pretty robust” guardrails to ensure the project cannot spring back to life.

Such caution was backed by other reporting. Reuters cited a source as saying Trump “is ‘not thrilled,’ but understands [putting the fund on hold] is the only way forward … ‘for now.’”

After our report on Monday about the Democrats who marched alongside genocidal Israeli officials at New York City’s Israel Day Parade, a handful of Democrats belatedly condemned the most prominent extremist attendee: Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich, for whose arrest the International Criminal Court is reportedly seeking a warrant.

Rep. Dan Goldman of New York told Zeteo: “I was unaware that Bezalel Smotrich attended the parade [on Sunday], nor did I see him, and I am disgusted that he was there. He does not reflect the Israel I know and support, and I have repeatedly called for his removal from office. My guiding light has always been for Israelis and Palestinians to live side by side in peace, security and self-determination, and Smotrich is a destructive obstacle to that outcome.”

New York Governor Kathy Hochul called Smotrich “a far-right extremist whose hateful and divisive rhetoric is fundamentally at odds with the values we hold dear in New York. [The] parade was a celebration of Jewish pride, community, and unity. I strongly condemn his participation.”

Attorney General Letitia James said: “Islamophobia has no place in New York. I unequivocally condemn Bezalel Smotrich’s hateful rhetoric.”

State Assemblyman (and congressional candidate) Alex Bores tweeted: “It’s alarming that… an extremist with a long history of racist, Islamophobic, and homophobic beliefs – and even comments denigrating Reform Jews – came to this parade. Smotrich has called for Palestinian villages to be ‘wiped out’ and has promoted aggressive settlement expansion. His views and actions disgust me and are antithetical to our values as New Yorkers and Americans.”

We await other responses, though some might say they’re too little, and way too late.

  • Anti-DEI:Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth blocked promotions for seven military officers, two of whom were women and two of whom were Black, theNew York Timesreported. The Pentagon denied the moves were motivated by Hegseth’s anti-diversity policies. In court, a judgestoppedHegseth from removing transgender troops but upheld a ban on recruitment, for now.
  • Boat strikes:TheTimesalsonotedthat more than 200 people have now been killed in strikes on boats the U.S. claims were carrying drugs in the Caribbean and Pacific. Experts say the strikes are illegal extra-judicial killings.
  • Press ganged:The Pentagon has made its press office a classified space and banned journalists from entering it, theWashington Postreported.
  • Unreformed:Tina Peters, the Colorado clerk jailed for helping Trump’s election subversion but granted clemency by Democratic Governor Jared Polis, left prison – and promptlytoldSteve Bannon that Democrats will cheat in the midterms.
  • Ban bashed:A UK government ban on U.S. progressives Cenk Uygur and Hasan Piker over their views on Israel was widelycondemned. Jemima Steinfeld, CEO of Index on Censorship, called the decision “paternalistic.” Akiko Hart, director of Liberty, said: “Free speech can only exist when we defend it for those we disagree with.”
  • Stormy Monday:After high-profile firings, veteran ‘60 Minutes’ anchor Scott Pelley told CBS News bosses editor-in-chief Bari Weiss was “murdering” the venerable news show, in service of Trump-allied boss David Ellison, multiple outletsreported. Experienced CBS journalistswroteto Ellison to demand he commit to 60 Minutes’ editorial independence.

In 1999, Pete Hegseth and Meredith Schwartz’s Minneapolis high-school class voted them “most likely to marry.” In 2004, they did so. When they divorced in 2008, how many affairs did Hegseth admit to?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:A ship carrying humanitarian aid for Gaza, the Handala II,set sailfrom Sweden. Israel has repeatedly and illegally intercepted such vessels, kidnapping, detaining, andbrutalizingactivists who crew them.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Ukraine hit:The mayor of Kyiv, former boxing champion Vitali Klitschko,reporteda major Russian missile attack early on Tuesday, as thousands took refuge in Metro stations and other shelters. The attacks on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities killed at least 16 people, APreported.

  • 🇲🇽

    Mexican standoff: President Claudia Sheinbaum stepped up rhetoric against American interference, telling reporters “sectors ​of the far right in the United States… want ​a bad relationship with Mexico” because of “ideological” differences. She also said she does not blame Donald Trump.
  • 🇬🇧 Epstein news:Filesrelated to the disastrous appointment of Jeffrey Epstein friend Peter Mandelson as UK ambassador to the U.S. revealed a note from Mandelson to then-Foreign Secretary David Lammy: “I just wanted you to know that if you were minded to appoint me, I would make sure you never regret it.” Safe to say, Lammy regrets it.
  • 🇨🇩🇺🇬 Ebola update:Health authoritiessaid264 cases of arare form of Ebolahad been confirmed in DR Congo, resulting in 42 deaths. Uganda has reported nine cases and closed its border.


No, your ears don’t deceive you. This is the chair of the Minnesota GOP, Alex Plechash, going on WCCO radio in Minneapolis to defend his party’s decision to observe a moment of silence for Derek Chauvin – the police officer who was convicted of murder and pleaded guilty to civil rights charges in the 2020 death of George Floyd, a killing that set off global protests for racial justice.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Prem Thakker contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

According to sources with direct knowledge of the situation, too many of the people Trump had asked about finding the leakers had themselves, well, leaked to the Regime Change co-authors.

Trump’s leak probes are no joke: They’re part of a much broader pattern of waging blatantly authoritarian warfare against free speech, the free press, whistleblowers, and liberal dissent in this country.

And yet I cannot possibly pretend that it isn’t hysterically funny how Trump has put himself in a position where it’s very hard for him to find the leakers because the senior personnel he tapped to be the leak-hunters-in-chief are also the ones who’ve been doing the leaking. Good help is hard to find!

When asked bluntly by Zeteo if they snitched, two senior officials, one current and one former, said, of course, they talked to Haberman and Swan for the book. Each said they were comfortable anonymously admitting it because that admission, by their own estimations, could have come from basically anybody at their level.

“It’s one of the worst-kept secrets at the White House,” the current official says, adding: “I’m not ratting myself out; that would be ridiculous.”

Another adviser to the president – who refused to say whether they had or hadn’t leaked to Haberman and Swan – compared the Trump-ordered leak hunt to the scene at the end of the Quentin Tarantino movie ‘Reservoir Dogs,’ in which the different characters have loaded guns pointed at one another, ensuring mutual destruction. (Your ‘First Draft’ author, personally, would analogize the situation to the 2007 film ‘Breach,’ when the Laura Linney character reveals that when the FBI wanted to find out who at the bureau was spying for the Soviets, it tasked agent Robert Hanssen with the mole hunt.)

The president, for his part, “has his suspicions” about who leaked certain details, the Trump adviser tells me. This person declined to elaborate.

Naturally, Zeteo can’t ethically out our own sources, who did not, even under the cloak of anonymity, want to provide reportable information that named names. However, you – as a shrewd observer of American politics – can feel confident that if you think of a name of a prominent Team Trump personality, you’re thinking of somebody who blabbed to the Times duo.

In response to a comment request for this story, Trump White House spokesman Davis Ingle would only reply: “What the hell is a Zeteo?” (The White House knows who we are; they have repeatedly called us out by name in public statements, blasted Mehdi as our “nutjob founder,” and dubbed me a “deranged hack” in the recent past. But thanks for the comment, guys! 😀)

Regime Change is due out late this month. Trump is incredibly pissed off about it. Expect his fury to billow up even more as we get closer to the publication date. And to repeat a disclosure I’ve made here at Zeteo before: Swan was one of my groomsmen, and I love him like a brother. So, Mr. Trump, if you’re listening: If you put him in the gulag for his “treason,” just remember that I’ve never met Jonathan, I know nothing of this “Swan” fellow, and I have no idea what you’re talking about… Thanks.

  • Talks chaos:Trump said Iran “really wants to make a deal.” But then the semi-official Tasnim news agencysaidIran was considering closing the Strait of Hormuz and other aggressive measures, while Iranian Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi, referring to Israeli strikes in Lebanon,said: “Violation on one front is a violation of the ceasefire on all fronts. The U.S. and Israel are responsible for the consequences of any violation.”
  • Lebanon latest:Later, Trumpsaidhe had secured agreement from Benjamin Netanyahu and Hezbollah “that all shooting will stop” in Lebanon, with Israel backing off from attacking Beirut. Lebanonconfirmeda partial ceasefire, but Netanyahu vowed that strikes would continue in the south, where at least eight people have been killed in Israeli attacks since yesterday, including an attack on a hospital. The death tollclimbedto at least 3,433 people.
  • Expletives undeleted:According toAxios, Trump’s call with Netanyahu was not full of diplomatic niceties. A U.S. official said Trump told the Israeli PM: “You’re fucking crazy. You’d be in prison if it weren’t for me. I’m saving your ass. Everybody hates you now. Everybody hates Israel because of this.” Trump then posted that talks to end the war were continuing at “a rapid pace.”
  • ‘Couldn’t care less’:Trump alsotoldCNBC talks with Iran were “very boring” and said: “I really don’t care. I couldn’t care less. If they’re over, they’re over.” The White House put out a postsayingpeople should just “TRUST IN TRUMP.”
  • Prices wrong:Unsurprisingly, amid so much uncertainty and erratic behavior, oil pricessurgedyet again.

Trump’s hugely controversial “Anti-Weaponization” slush fund is not dead yet – but the White House has put it on hold.

First, Axios scooped that the administration “planned to drop” the proposed $1.776 billion fund, announced last month by acting Attorney General Todd Blanche and the cause of immediate outrage over the prospect of payouts to Trump supporters supposedly victimized by law enforcement, including election deniers and rioters who attacked the U.S. Capitol on January 6.

Trump was also given broad immunity from investigation by the Internal Revenue Service, the subject of his spurious lawsuit, which was “settled” to create the fund.

Democrats and Republicans alike were outraged by such naked corruption. But on Monday, tellingly, one source told Axios such backlash merely meant the fund was “dead for now.” Nothing, the outlet noted, “is certain in the Trump administration until it’s officially announced or the president says it himself.”

Later, the Department of Justice released a statement attacking a Virginia judge’s ruling last week that paused the fund until mid-June, but said it would respect the order. The statement also claimed the fund, meant to be governed by DOJ and Trump appointees, would have been “open to anybody who was so weaponized, targeted, or persecuted, whether they were Democrat, Republican, Conservative, Independent, or otherwise.”

To reach for a technical term: Yeah, right.

Notably, not even Trump’s own party is convinced he will not try to revive the fund. Senator John Curtis of Utah told the  New York Times he had “a lot of unanswered questions” and wanted “pretty robust” guardrails to ensure the project cannot spring back to life.

Such caution was backed by other reporting. Reuters cited a source as saying Trump “is ‘not thrilled,’ but understands [putting the fund on hold] is the only way forward … ‘for now.’”

After our report on Monday about the Democrats who marched alongside genocidal Israeli officials at New York City’s Israel Day Parade, a handful of Democrats belatedly condemned the most prominent extremist attendee: Israeli Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich, for whose arrest the International Criminal Court is reportedly seeking a warrant.

Rep. Dan Goldman of New York told Zeteo: “I was unaware that Bezalel Smotrich attended the parade [on Sunday], nor did I see him, and I am disgusted that he was there. He does not reflect the Israel I know and support, and I have repeatedly called for his removal from office. My guiding light has always been for Israelis and Palestinians to live side by side in peace, security and self-determination, and Smotrich is a destructive obstacle to that outcome.”

New York Governor Kathy Hochul called Smotrich “a far-right extremist whose hateful and divisive rhetoric is fundamentally at odds with the values we hold dear in New York. [The] parade was a celebration of Jewish pride, community, and unity. I strongly condemn his participation.”

Attorney General Letitia James said: “Islamophobia has no place in New York. I unequivocally condemn Bezalel Smotrich’s hateful rhetoric.”

State Assemblyman (and congressional candidate) Alex Bores tweeted: “It’s alarming that… an extremist with a long history of racist, Islamophobic, and homophobic beliefs – and even comments denigrating Reform Jews – came to this parade. Smotrich has called for Palestinian villages to be ‘wiped out’ and has promoted aggressive settlement expansion. His views and actions disgust me and are antithetical to our values as New Yorkers and Americans.”

We await other responses, though some might say they’re too little, and way too late.

  • Anti-DEI:Secretary of Defense Pete Hegseth blocked promotions for seven military officers, two of whom were women and two of whom were Black, theNew York Timesreported. The Pentagon denied the moves were motivated by Hegseth’s anti-diversity policies. In court, a judgestoppedHegseth from removing transgender troops but upheld a ban on recruitment, for now.
  • Boat strikes:TheTimesalsonotedthat more than 200 people have now been killed in strikes on boats the U.S. claims were carrying drugs in the Caribbean and Pacific. Experts say the strikes are illegal extra-judicial killings.
  • Press ganged:The Pentagon has made its press office a classified space and banned journalists from entering it, theWashington Postreported.
  • Unreformed:Tina Peters, the Colorado clerk jailed for helping Trump’s election subversion but granted clemency by Democratic Governor Jared Polis, left prison – and promptlytoldSteve Bannon that Democrats will cheat in the midterms.
  • Ban bashed:A UK government ban on U.S. progressives Cenk Uygur and Hasan Piker over their views on Israel was widelycondemned. Jemima Steinfeld, CEO of Index on Censorship, called the decision “paternalistic.” Akiko Hart, director of Liberty, said: “Free speech can only exist when we defend it for those we disagree with.”
  • Stormy Monday:After high-profile firings, veteran ‘60 Minutes’ anchor Scott Pelley told CBS News bosses editor-in-chief Bari Weiss was “murdering” the venerable news show, in service of Trump-allied boss David Ellison, multiple outletsreported. Experienced CBS journalistswroteto Ellison to demand he commit to 60 Minutes’ editorial independence.

In 1999, Pete Hegseth and Meredith Schwartz’s Minneapolis high-school class voted them “most likely to marry.” In 2004, they did so. When they divorced in 2008, how many affairs did Hegseth admit to?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:A ship carrying humanitarian aid for Gaza, the Handala II,set sailfrom Sweden. Israel has repeatedly and illegally intercepted such vessels, kidnapping, detaining, andbrutalizingactivists who crew them.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Ukraine hit:The mayor of Kyiv, former boxing champion Vitali Klitschko,reporteda major Russian missile attack early on Tuesday, as thousands took refuge in Metro stations and other shelters. The attacks on Kyiv and other Ukrainian cities killed at least 16 people, APreported.

  • 🇲🇽

    Mexican standoff: President Claudia Sheinbaum stepped up rhetoric against American interference, telling reporters “sectors ​of the far right in the United States… want ​a bad relationship with Mexico” because of “ideological” differences. She also said she does not blame Donald Trump.
  • 🇬🇧 Epstein news:Filesrelated to the disastrous appointment of Jeffrey Epstein friend Peter Mandelson as UK ambassador to the U.S. revealed a note from Mandelson to then-Foreign Secretary David Lammy: “I just wanted you to know that if you were minded to appoint me, I would make sure you never regret it.” Safe to say, Lammy regrets it.
  • 🇨🇩🇺🇬 Ebola update:Health authoritiessaid264 cases of arare form of Ebolahad been confirmed in DR Congo, resulting in 42 deaths. Uganda has reported nine cases and closed its border.


No, your ears don’t deceive you. This is the chair of the Minnesota GOP, Alex Plechash, going on WCCO radio in Minneapolis to defend his party’s decision to observe a moment of silence for Derek Chauvin – the police officer who was convicted of murder and pleaded guilty to civil rights charges in the 2020 death of George Floyd, a killing that set off global protests for racial justice.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly and Prem Thakker contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

item.title: California Progressives Bet on Billionaire ‘Class Traitor’
item.description: On a Saturday afternoon near San Francisco’s Union Square, around 400 supporters of Tom Steyer’s gubernatorial campaign assembled, many wearing the same accessory: khaki hats that read “class traitor” on the front, and “Tom Steyer is a…” on the back. The hats rounded out a recurring theme among…
item.author: "Zeteo | U.S. Politics"
item.content:encoded:
On a Saturday afternoon near San Francisco’s Union Square, around 400 supporters of Tom Steyer’s gubernatorial campaign assembled, many wearing the same accessory: khaki hats that read “class traitor” on the front, and “Tom Steyer is a…” on the back.

The hats rounded out a recurring theme among Steyer’s decisively left-leaning coalition: They aren’t shy about his status as a billionaire – they own the attention this draws, and redirect it at his policies, which include single-payer healthcare and a billionaire tax.

Three days out from the California primary, Steyer addressed the energized crowd while standing in front of union representatives, construction workers, and other supporters holding signs that read, “abolish ICE,” “tax billionaires,” and “make polluters pay.”

“I wasn’t born a billionaire,” Steyer said, “and I won’t die a billionaire. As governor, I’ll raise taxes on billionaires. I’m the only candidate who will say that – it’s the reason the other billionaires hate me.”

Steyer pointed to record-levels of outside spending against him in the race, and directly quoting FDR, he drew similarities between themselves: “Never before in all our history have these forces been so united against one candidate as they stand today. They are unanimous in their hate for me – and I welcome their hatred.”

He performed a roll call of corporations that have contributed roughly $50 million to boost his opponent Xavier Becerra’s campaign, from Chevron and Facebook’s parent company Meta, to the electric utility PG&E and McDonald’s – prompting one audience member to loudly dub Becerra the “Hamburglar.”

Steyer’s remarks were preceded by former state comptroller Betty Yee, Congresswoman Lateefah Simon, California Nurses Association President Michelle Gutierrez Vo, and Susana Williams of the Bernie Sanders-aligned Our Revolution. Williams, visibly emotional, compared Steyer’s campaign to Sanders’ presidential bid in 2016. Yee praised Steyer’s “diverse coalition,” while Simon described this coalition as one of “healers and builders.”

In an outcome that would be baffling without crucial context – namely, Steyer’s policy positions – the billionaire has amassed an unlikely base of progressive leaders, with endorsements from national, left-leaning icons like Mark Ruffalo and Rep. Ro Khanna. But where Gavin Newsom cruised to victory in 2018 and again in 2022, this year’s race has seen a hotly contested, even vitriolic primary for Democrats – with Steyer’s status as a billionaire often taking center-stage. At times, friction and division among the crowded Democratic field have even fueled concerns that two Republican candidates could sweep the top two spots in the primary and lock Democrats out of the general election, in one of the most reliably liberal states in the nation.

Since last fall, the governor’s race has seen one curveball after another: From Katie Porter’s hostile workplace practices being videotaped, to long-simmering sexual misconduct scandals forcing Eric Swalwell to suspend his campaign in April, when he once appeared to be a frontrunner.

But since Swalwell bowed out, the primary has largely come down to Republican Steve Hilton, Steyer, and Becerra – the latter of whom polled at just around 5% as recently as February. Today, Steyer and Becerra are polling neck-and-neck in a statistical dead heat, with polls in the final days giving Becerra an edge going into Tuesday.

But the most recent polls have been all over the place: November could feature Becerra or Steyer versus a Republican, or the two Democrats might even clinch the top spots and lock Republicans out of the general.

Either way, Tuesday makes for a compelling showdown between a progressive billionaire versus a corporate Democrat.

Speaking to Zeteo after his Saturday remarks, Steyer described the primary as “a real question about who California is going to serve: Are we going to make the government work for working people, make their lives better, or are we going to continue to let corporations run this state?”

Days before Steyer’s Saturday rally, Khanna endorsed the billionaire at a town hall at the Luther Burbank Center for the Arts in Santa Rosa, speaking to an over-capacity crowd of over 600. On a press call the following day, Khanna described the race between Steyer and Becerra as “a coin flip.”

On the same press call, which saw Steyer’s campaign introduce him as “the only billionaire on the ballot – but not the only one in the race,” a question from an ABC7 reporter challenged this framing: “At what point does personal wealth give someone an unfair advantage in a democratic election? Some voters are uncomfortable with that level of spending – are they wrong?”

Steyer has spent an estimated $213 million on his campaign. You can hardly turn on a television in the state of California without glimpsing ads from him – pledging to build affordable housing, break up monopolies, regulate AI, and jail ICE agents. “Sacramento politicians are afraid to change up the system. They’re going to hate this. Bring it on,” he declares in one ad.

A group called California is Not for Sale PAC has been bankrolling anti-Steyers ads – and despite the PAC’s branding, it’s funded by corporations like PG&E, the largest utility monopoly in the state, as well as the California Chamber of Commerce and the state’s realtors association. (Thanks to the state’s election laws, those names are disclosed in the group’s ads – right next to its ironic messaging about how “California is not for sale.”)

Becerra has received significant support from Chevron, Meta, Uber, AirBnb, health insurer Elevance (formerly known as Anthem), PG&E, prediction market Kalshi, and other corporations. By contrast, Steyer’s coalition includes endorsements from Our Revolution, environmental group Sierra Club, the Jane Fonda Climate PAC, the California Teachers’ Association, and more.

Steyer and Becerra’s showdown builds on ongoing tensions within the Democratic Party base over the limits of liberal identity politics. Today, progressives prioritize policies like single-payer healthcare, a billionaire tax, bold climate action, getting corporate PACs out of politics, unwavering support for reproductive freedom and LGBTQ rights, and other tangible policies – even if they come from a straight, white, male billionaire.

Or, as Steyer put it in his Saturday remarks: “You’re not voting for who’s on the ballot. You’re voting for the California that comes after it.”

Speaking to Zeteo, Our Revolution executive director Joseph Geevarghese stressed that he and most members of his organizations “don’t believe we should have billionaires” and regard their existence as “a policy failure.”

“On paper, we make for strange bedfellows – why is an anti-billionaire group endorsing a billionaire?” Geevarghese said. “But our movement should be based on ideology, not identity.” And “one proof point” of Steyer’s progressive bona fides, he argues, is “the corporate opposition aligning themselves against Steyer, around Becerra – because they want a status quo candidate.”

Steyer’s progressive positions haven’t made him impervious to questions about his massive personal wealth. But his campaign and its surrogates have by now grown quite adept at addressing these contradictions.

In response to ABC7’s question about Steyer having an “unfair advantage” on the Thursday press call, California Assembly Progressive Caucus Chair Alex Lee, a campaign surrogate, acknowledged that “people have the right to be skeptical of billionaires entering politics and using that kind of money for their own campaign.”

But he added, “In many ways, Tom is really fighting fire with fire. His ability to shoot back and fight back corporate powers is really what freaks them out so much.”

Steyer’s campaign spending, Lee said, exposes “the great irony” that progressive candidates are otherwise “buried beneath massive corporate spending,” like the contributions that corporations have guzzled into Becerra’s campaign and PACs that are aligned with it. Khanna echoed this response, arguing that until the Supreme Court’s Citizens United ruling, which enables nearly unfettered corporate spending in elections, is overruled, this is the system we’re working within – a system that Khanna believes Steyer will help dismantle.

“I really do believe Tom will be our strong progressive governor, even if people are skeptical,” Lee said. “Sometimes, a billionaire can be fighting for the working class, while we have lots of candidates that are beholden to billionaires, to corporations.”

Despite Steyer’s surprising popularity (or, at least, measured support) among the left, his critics – often Becerra supporters or conservatives – tend to write Steyer off as the caricature of the “champagne socialist.”

This line of attack is hardly limited to Steyer: When conservatives disagree with a left-leaning, wealthy person, they rarely engage on the substance of the issues, and instead simply write them off for being rich.

This has been the right’s response to vocally left-leaning celebrities like Billie Eillish and Chappell Roan; activists like Hasan Piker; and politicians like Sanders and Alexandria Ocasio-Cortez, for daring to own a home or wear nice clothes.

Of course, if someone has more to lose than gain from their political positions, shouldn’t this bolster rather than weaken their credibility?

“I’ve been working on these issues full-time for 14 years,” Steyer told Zeteo. “My run for governor is completely consistent with my values and my family’s values. I try not to pay attention to people who tell me who I am, and just be the person I actually am.”

Geevarghese says he sees some benefits to a billionaire like Steyer being the one to tell corporate CEOs that policies like single-payer “are reasonable, doable, and efficient.”

As we operate within a political system that Geevarghese ultimately hopes to tear down, he believes a progressive billionaire might have more credibility among skeptical factions “than a more traditional grassroots candidate.”

“There’s a part of me that sees it as badass that a billionaire is willing to take on the billionaire class and be a class traitor,” Geevarghese said. In order to win and upend the system, “you do have to fragment the power structure – one way for us to win is to create cleavages within the billionaire class.”

In addition to politicians like Khanna and Lee, other key players in leftist politics have taken a keen interest in Steyer’s campaign – like Piker, a California resident who hasn’t endorsed Steyer, but had him on his stream in March, and commentator Jennifer Welch, who had Steyer on her ‘I’ve Had It’ podcast last week. One clip of Steyer passionately defending trans kids in sports has since drawn over 3 million views.

Viral moments with popular influencers like Piker and Welch have played a major role in a distinctly online race like this one. Social media has erupted into a contentious battleground for Steyer and Becerra: Influencer-surrogates for both campaigns have gone back-and-forth over the last month, accusing each other of receiving undisclosed payments in exchange for supportive social media content, and failing to be transparent. (A 2023 state law requires influencers to disclose whether they’ve been paid by a campaign to produce content either for or against a candidate.) Both campaigns deny wrongdoing.

Per the Los Angeles Times, Steyer’s campaign has paid some influencers thousands of dollars for supportive content; a spokesperson told the outlet: “The campaign believes in compensating people for their time and work product and has paid creators to generate content. Payments for creator content are disclosed in campaign finance reports, and we notify creators we directly work with of their disclosure requirements.”

But influencers aren’t the only matter at issue: The potentially outsized role of bots has also raised questions. Cyabra, an information security company, last week released a report commissioned by the Steyer campaign alleging more than 3,000 fake accounts across Instagram, Twitter, and Facebook are boosting Becerra’s posts while attacking Steyer. Per the report, these bots drove over a million views and around 42,000 engagements – possibly creating the image of more excitement for Becerra than there is in reality.

The role of paid influencers and concerns about digital transparency are hardly unique to this particular race – and they aren’t going anywhere any time soon. In a country that spends the GDP of a small nation each election cycle, it’s becoming harder every day for voters to discern what’s coming to them authentically from astroturfing and political spon-con.

For all the attention Steyer’s status as a billionaire has received in this race, his opponents aren’t without their own controversies. San Jose Mayor Matt Mahan has come under fire for his ties to the Peter Thiel-founded spy tech firm Palantir and Big Tech writ large. Exasperated progressives have been pleading with Porter to drop out of the race for months – over her treatment of staff, and concerns she’s splitting the progressive vote.

Then, there’s Becerra: Support for his campaign among dodgy corporations has become a meme on Twitter. And while he’s drawn weighty congressional endorsements from Democrats like Reps. Jasmine Crockett and Ted Lieu, Becerra’s former Biden administration colleagues have called him “an embarrassment,” “not prepared at all,” and “an unfortunate choice” to lead Biden’s Health and Human Services Department.

Susan Rice, U.S. ambassador to the United Nations under Biden, famously regarded Becerra as incompetent, even calling him an “idiot.”

The Intercept has reported on Becerra’s tenure as attorney general of California, which saw his office attempt to inflate the IQ of a Black man on death row to push for the death penalty. The New York Times has reported on Becerra’s tenure at HHS, during which his office struggled to keep track of roughly 85,000 immigrant children who’d just crossed the border, leading to “serious breakdowns in how HHS vetted sponsors and safeguarded children.” Becerra has written off this reporting as “Trump lies,” while his surrogates have continued to hammer Steyer on his being a billionaire.

Yet, among some members of Steyer’s base, this statement of fact – that Steyer is a billionaire – reinforces the very crux of why they support him: They regard him as a class traitor endowed with unique, firsthand knowledge of the enemy.

Steyer has embraced this read. “I see the way so many billionaires lie, cheat, and steal to get whatever they can out of the system,” he told supporters on Saturday. “Screw that.”

item.title: ⚽🏆 The Dark Side of The World Cup: Episode 1
item.description: Millions around the world are getting ready for the most-watched sporting event on the planet – the FIFA World Cup, which will be mostly taking place in the United States. Though World Cups tend to create a festival atmosphere associated with joy and excitement, the darker forces operating behind…
item.author: "Zeteo | Dark Side of the World Cup "
item.content:encoded:
Millions around the world are getting ready for the most-watched sporting event on the planet – the FIFA World Cup, which will be mostly taking place in the United States.

Though World Cups tend to create a festival atmosphere associated with joy and excitement, the darker forces operating behind the scenes tend only to be highlighted when the tournament is happening in certain parts of the world.

At Zeteo, we feel it’s important to talk about the sinister side of the World Cup wherever it’s happening. That’s what makes us, us. So, over the coming weeks, we’re going to take you on a journey through ‘The Dark Side of the World Cup’ – full of history, scandal and absurdity – in three investigative deep-dives.

Our first episode? ‘The Long Trump Shadow’

We have to start this year’s tournament with Donald Trump, who is using the World Cup to feed his ego (no surprise!) but also to distract us from his various misdeeds and crimes behind the scenes.

Don’t miss out on Episode 1, in which Mehdi breaks down how Trump has made the tournament all about himself with the aid of experts who unpack FIFA’s complicity, the Western world’s hypocrisy, and how ICE might play a dark role at this tournament.

There’s no better way to get ready for the World Cup than to understand what’s been happening in the circles of power around it.

Paid subscribers can watch the full episode, without a paywall, above.

Free subscribers can watch a 3-minute preview. Consider becoming a paid subscriber to Zeteo to watch the full episode, the next two episodes, and all of the rest of Zeteo’s award-winning and original content.

Let us know what you thought in the comments! And stay tuned for Episodes 2 and 3 coming very soon.

Check out Zeteo’s other recent stories:

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.

item.title: This Is How the Iran War Has Exposed Israel's Weakness in the Middle East
item.description: To understand the policies of Benjamin Netanyahu’s government, we must recall that the Israeli prime minister is the political heir of Ze’ev Jabotinsky, the founder of the Revisionist Zionist movement, one of whose aides was Netanyahu’s father , Benzion. Jabotinsky outlined Israel’s stark reality…
item.author: Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
To understand the policies of Benjamin Netanyahu’s government, we must recall that the Israeli prime minister is the political heir of Ze’ev Jabotinsky, the founder of the Revisionist Zionist movement, one of whose aides was Netanyahu’s father, Benzion. Jabotinsky outlined Israel’s stark reality in an essay in 1923 titled “The Iron Wall.”

He argued that Jewish “colonization” of Palestine (and Jabotinsky had no hesitation in using that term) could only proceed behind an “iron wall” of Zionist military superiority. He accepted that Palestinian Arabs would, like “every native population in the world [resist] colonists as long as it has the slightest hope of being able to rid itself of the danger of being colonized.” This led Jabotinsky to conclude that the only way Arabs would ever agree to a Jewish presence in Palestine “is the iron wall, which is to say a strong power in Palestine that is not amenable to any Arab pressure. In other words, the only way to reach an agreement in the future is to abandon all idea of seeking an agreement at present.”

In many regards, this is the policy Netanyahu has long implemented – one of relying on Israel’s military supremacy and only really entering into agreements that consolidate this supremacy. But “an agreement in the future” can be a woolly notion if military superiority allows a country to continually put off the compromises necessary to bring about settlements.

This has led to an Israeli attitude that Carnegie scholar Nathan J. Brown has pithily described as “one of dominance without settlement, degradation without reconstruction, territorial denial without a political horizon, and war not as a bridge to an arrangement but the arrangement itself.”

These thoughts came to my mind on May 23, in reading a tweet by Danny Citrinowicz, a former head of the Iran branch in the Research and Analysis Division of Israeli defense intelligence, who has written lucidly and critically of the U.S. and Israeli war against Iran. Citrinowicz remarked that Netanyahu regarded the possible U.S. agreement with Iran being negotiated as a “personal and political defeat,” because, “[h]is approach was based on the belief that more pressure, more military power, and tighter coordination between Israel and the United States would eventually either force Iran into submission or destabilize the regime itself.”

Citrinowicz was not wrong, but he left something out of his analysis. Those mediating in the effort to persuade President Donald Trump to end military operations against Iran were members of a coalition of countries – Saudi Arabia, Turkey, Qatar, Egypt, and Pakistan – who are not only worried about what a continuation of the war might mean for their economies and regional stability, but who are also united in seeking to contain Israel, which they believe wants to impose its hegemony in the Middle East, supported by the United States.

What we’re seeing in the region is the emergence of a traditional balance-of-power mechanism. This is realpolitik, not personal, and if today the perceived danger is Israel, new coalitions could easily form again against other powers trying to achieve regional domination. While none of these countries is particularly sympathetic to Iran, which many once saw in the same way as they presently do Israel, they share with the Iranians a parallel interest in pushing back against Israel.

This willingness to take Iranian interests into consideration is partly what led Egypt, in November 2025, to come up with a new formula to address Hezbollah’s weapons. Last summer, Lebanon’s government decided the state should have a monopoly over weapons, meaning Hezbollah should be disarmed. Realizing this could lead to domestic conflict, as the party and its sponsor, Iran, rejected such an outcome, the Egyptians floated a compromise for “containment” of the weapons. The state would not disarm Hezbollah, but there would be an agreement to store the weapons and only use them as a deterrent against Israel.

Such dynamics show what Israel is up against in assuming it can sustain an iron wall approach to the region. It can surely continue to occupy and destroy parts of Lebanon, Gaza, and Syria, and call the ensuing wasteland peace; it can continue to try to ethnically cleanse Palestinians in Gaza and the West Bank; but the regional environment is bringing out the fundamental problem in the iron wall concept, namely that it allows Israel to defer political agreements to an indefinite, even unwanted, future. Its reliance on prodigious force, its refusal to cash in on this by securing stable political outcomes, facilitates the continuous emergence of adversaries who regard Israel as a menace and, therefore, will collaborate against it.

Perpetual war won’t bring perpetual peace for Israel. If anything, it will produce a more dangerous strategic reality. In the past, the Israelis deployed crushing military power to impose peace agreements on some of their neighbors, while ignoring the Palestinians entirely. Whatever the wisdom of such an approach, it did have a clear political endgame. Yet mass murder in Gaza brought about a new reality, namely that Middle Eastern publics now view Israel as an intolerable rogue state, unworthy of peace, reinforcing attitudes among their regimes that now also see it as a strategic threat.

Citrinowicz summarized the dilemma well: “Israel’s confrontation with Iran will not bring normalization with the Arab world, nor will it resolve Israel’s most fundamental security challenges, first and foremost, the Palestinian issue.” Instead, Israel has generated parallel interests among Saudi Arabia, Turkey, and Egypt as well, which, even if they seek balance, not open conflict, will make it much costlier in the future for the Israelis to advance their aims behind an iron wall of violence.

Last month, in a conversation with Arab leaders, several from the coalition forming against Israel, Trump asked them to join the Abraham Accords. This showed how out of touch he was with realities in the Middle East. Regional leaders believe that once U.S. officials assess the magnitude of the Iran fiasco, and Israel’s key role in bringing it about, they may be more predisposed to disengage from the region. This could leave Israel strategically isolated. If so, Arab states and Turkey would be in a better position to negotiate peace settlements that take their priorities into consideration.

Like Gaza, the Iran war is a turning point, one that should make Israelis think twice.

Michael Young is a senior editor at the Malcolm H. Kerr Carnegie Middle East Center in Beirut and editor of Diwan, Carnegie’s Middle East blog. Previously, he was opinion editor, as well as a columnist, for the Daily Star newspaper in Lebanon. He is the author of The Ghosts of Martyrs Square: An Eyewitness Account of Lebanon’s Life Struggle (Simon & Schuster, 2010).

item.title: Why Did Top Democrats Just Attend a Genocide Parade?
item.description: On this day in 1980, CNN made its debut as the world’s first 24-hour news network, a model that would change the face of TV newscasting – for the worse, critics say. Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday wherever you are. Happy June! Today I’m grateful for the gift of…
item.author: First Draft | Zeteo
item.content:encoded:
On this day in 1980, CNN made its debut as the world’s first 24-hour news network, a model that would change the face of TV newscasting – for the worse, critics say.

Good morning, friends. Prem here. Wishing you a wonderful Monday wherever you are. Happy June! Today I’m grateful for the gift of good sport. Seeing Arsenal vs. PSG go to penalties (sorry, Mayor Mamdani), and the young San Antonio Spurs surviving against the Oklahoma City Thunder was a blast. I’m super-conflicted on Spurs vs. Knicks, though. Love Spurs basketball, but also love the prospect of an NYC win and

In today’s ‘First Draft,’ a breakdown of all the genocidal Israeli officials who were happily parading in New York City – and all the Democrats who marched alongside them. Plus, Hasan Piker and Cenk Uygur are banned by the UK over their views on Israel and Donald Trump posts yet more evidence of his unfitness for office for all the world to see.

Let’s dig in!

In October 2025, Republican mayoral candidate Curtis Sliwa uttered a fateful phrase. “Every parade has the right to exist in New York City.”

But does that mean the mayor has to go to every parade?

On Sunday, New York was host to the Israel Day Parade. Crowds of pro-Israel marchers, Christian Zionists, Israeli officials, and U.S. politicians descended on the Upper East Side.

Mayor Zohran Mamdani attracted vicious criticism for being the first New York mayor in more than 60 years not to attend the event, which honors an apartheid state internationally that is credibly accused of genocide.

Mamdani avoided marching alongside Israeli officials who have expressed extremist, genocidal, and viciously racist sentiments about Palestinians – and at least one for whom the International Criminal Court is reportedly seeking an arrest warrant.

That did not stop many other Democrats from marching. New York Senator Chuck Schumer, Governor Kathy Hochul, Attorney General Letitia James, Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch, Council Speaker Julie Menin, disgraced former Mayor Eric Adams, disgraced former Governor and failed mayoral candidate Andrew Cuomo, Rep. Dan Goldman, Rep. Jerry Nadler (and Alex Bores, vying to take Nadler’s seat) – were in attendance. New York Republican Rep. Mike Lawler joined them.

So, who were all these New Yorkers marching with? Let’s take a survey.

Among the most vile attendees was Israeli Finance Minister and self-proclaimed homophobe, racist, and fascist Bezalel Smotrich, a settler who has helped lead efforts to illegally annex more of the West Bank. The International Criminal Court has reportedly filed an arrest warrant application against the man who said it would be “right and moral” to starve 2 million Palestinians “to death.”

Joining Smotrich were:

  • Amichai Eliyahu, the heritage minister who has called fornukingandwiping outGaza, and forstarvingPalestinians.
  • Yitzhak Wasserlauf, minister for Negev and Galilee affairs, whostormedAl-Aqsa Mosque, one of the holiest sites in Islam, andtoldIsraeli soldiers to target babies.

  • Minister of Diaspora Affairs

    Amichai Chikli, whosoughtto ban Mamdani’s wife from Israel,saidIsrael should settle northern Gaza,bannedaid groups from Gaza, andboosteda pro-Nazi Romanian politician.

  • Minister of Aliyah and Integration

    Ofir Sofer, who hasworked with Smotrichto financially reward people who move into illegal settlements in the West Bank.

  • Knesset Speaker

    Amir Ohana,who hassaidMuslims are prone to“cultural murderousness,”orderedjails not to vaccinate Palestinian detainees for COVID-19, andcalledfor the release of a Jewish man arrested in connection with a deadly shooting of an Arab-Israeli man, even saying,“Law-abiding citizens carrying weapons are a force multiplier for the authorities for the immediate neutralization of threat and danger.”
  • Meirav Ben Ari,an opposition politician who in October 2023said, as Israel attacked,“The children in Gaza have brought this upon themselves!”
  • Ariel Kallner, a Likud politician who hassaid,“Gazans are plump and healthy; it’s all nonsense;” “Whoever remains in the north [Gaza] – we will truly starve them;”and, in October 2023, “Right now, one goal: Nakba! A Nakba that will overshadow the Nakba of 48. Nakba in Gaza and Nakba to anyone who dares to join!”

… and more.

Also in attendance was Israeli Ambassador to the UN Danny Danon, who Benjamin Netanyahu once fired after Danon criticized him for “leftist feebleness” during the 2014 Gaza War, which killed some 2,300 Palestinians and wounded 10,000.

“This encounter at the Israel March says it all,” Danon posted. “An NYPD cop, a proud Jew with a kippah, representing the incredible mosaic of the community in New York and the uncompromising support for Israel. 🇺🇸🇮🇱.”

Unwittingly, that embodied the actual message of the day: that the NYPD wasn’t pursuing any of the allegedly genocidal officials in attendance.

Could you imagine if there was a parade in which even one prominent attendee was a Muslim, for example, who had called for violence against an entire group of people? Would so many Democrats so obsequiously champion such an affair?

Mamdani’s absence was entirely reasonable – all the more so given some pro-Israel marchers were among crowds who just last week screamed to deport him.

Pro-Israel actors and media figures sought to slander Mamdani.

“It is worth noting that this is nothing more than a peaceful and positive celebration of the existence of the only Jewish state in the world,” the New York and New Jersey chapter of the Anti-Defamation League said – a quaint way to describe a parade headlined by a murderer’s row of genocidal politicians.

Beth Miller, political director of Jewish Voice for Peace, countered the pro-Israel narrative.

“It’s antisemitic to conflate Jews and Israel. Which is exactly what the ADL is doing by calling the ‘Israel Day Parade’ a ‘Jewish celebration,’” she wrote.

“As a Jewish person who lives here, I’m pretty fucking glad we finally have a mayor who isn’t at a parade celebrating atrocity crimes.”

  • Art of No Deal:Donald Trumpdemandedtougher terms for Iran after being presented with a deal to extend the ceasefire, thereby extending uncertainty over control of the Strait of Hormuz, Iran’s nuclear ambitions, and other issues.
  • New attacks:U.S. Central Commandsaidit bombed radar and drone sites in the Iranian city of Geruk and on Qeshm Island after Tehran downed a U.S. drone over the weekend. Iran then said it responded to an American attack by targeting a U.S. base in the region, likely referring to reported attacks on Kuwait.
  • Ship strike:The U.S. military alsosaidit stopped a vessel trying to break its blockade of Iranian ports by firing a missile into its engine room. The U.S. did not board the Gambian-flagged ship, which was set adrift in the Gulf of Oman. TheNew York Timessaidan unnamed official told it the U.S. had helped around 70 ships through the Strait of Hormuz in the last three weeks.
  • Lebanon latest:Continuing to ignore a supposed ceasefire and citing Hezbollah fire into Israel, Benjamin Netanyahuordereda deeper incursion into Lebanese territory andstrikeson Beirut’s southern suburbs. Israeli forces seized Beaufort Castle, a900-year-old Crusader fortress. The death toll from Israeli strikes on Lebanon hastopped3,370 people.

Young Turks founder Cenk Uygur and his nephew, progressive streamer Hasan Piker, have been banned from the UK.

“I tried to get on a flight to London to attend SXSW London and give a speech at Oxford,” Uygur said. “I’ve been banned for criticizing Israel. Are we free anymore? This is oppression of Western citizens by our own governments on behalf of a different country!”

The Times reported, in an article Uygur posted, that UK Home Secretary Shabana Mahmood revoked Uygur’s visa “after concern over comments about Israel and grooming gangs.”

Uygur said the British government labeled him “a serious risk to the public order” because it deemed his position on Israel and its donations to members of Congress antisemitic. “I didn’t get banned for criticizing the UK, but for criticizing Israel,” he said, adding: “This is absolutely Kafkaesque.”

He then discussed the issue on YouTube.

Uygur had been due to speak to SXSW London on Wednesday, on the subject “Techno-Feudalism is Here. Who Are the Lords?” Piker was due to speak on Thursday, about “How the American Left Learned to Speak the Internet.” He was also due to appear at the Oxford Union.

Telling followers, “My visa has also been revoked,” Piker said he had been set to appear with figures including former Greek Finance Minister Yanis Varoufakis, former Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn, and current Greens leader Zack Polanski.

But last week, David Taylor, a Labour MP, called for Piker to be barred, saying his visit would not be “conducive to the public good.” Taylor claimed Piker had “openly supported a proscribed terrorist organisation and spouted… vile antisemitic rants.”

Piker strongly denies antisemitism, pointing out that criticism of Israel and support for Palestinian rights do not equate to such prejudice.

Piker said his barring from the UK was illustrative of “a sad state of affairs, where obviously the interests of Israel take the highest priority.”

  • Slop spree:Donald Trump went on a social media tear on Saturday, posting more than 50 times, much of it AI-generated. Combining favorite targets, one post about a meeting between Leo XIV and Brandon Johnsonread: “Someone should explain to the Pope that the Mayor of Chicago is useless, and that Iran cannot have a Nuclear Weapon.”
  • Out of tune:Trump alsosaidconcerts marking the country’s 250th birthday should be canceled, after acts dropped out of the events planned for the National Mall, citing their affiliation with Trump’s White House. Trump said he might hold a rally instead, because he was “the Number One Attraction anywhere in the World,” attracting “much larger audiences than Elvis in his prime.”Really.
  • Cry for help:In a bizarre appeal, “MAGA Lefty” and News Nation host Batya Ungar-SargonbeggedTrump to help “people who voted for you three times, who would walk over Legos coated in hot coals to vote for you, [who] are broke.”
  • ICE blast:Democratic Senator Andy Kim, last weekpepper-sprayedat a protest outside a New Jersey ICE facility,saidDHS Secretary Markwayne Mullin’s threat to retaliate for such protests by withdrawing customs officials from airports in “sanctuary cities” would “do so much economic damage” and “made no sense.”
  • Texts leak:Maine Democratic Senate candidate Graham Platner’s wife, Amy Gertner,addressedreports she disclosed to the campaign that she once found sexually explicit text messages on her husband’s phone —callingthe leak a breach of trust and criticizing media outlets for spreading “gossip.” Platner leads GOP incumbent Susan Collins in polling.

Donald Trump recently said he never met Elvis Presley but did meet Frank Sinatra. According to manager Eliot Weisman, what did Sinatra tell Trump in 1990, when Trump tried to renegotiate a deal to perform at his Atlantic City casino?

Keep your eyes out for the answer below!

  • 🇵🇸 Don’t forget Gaza:An Israeli airstrike killed at least two Palestinians and wounded 12 at a cafe busy with Eid holiday customers, health officialssaid. Israel continues to ignore a supposed U.S.-brokered ceasefire, with around 900 Palestinians killed since its supposed implementation last October.

  • 🇺🇦🇷🇺

    Missiles appeal:PresidentVolodymyr Zelenskytold CBSUkraine needed U.S. anti-ballistic missiles to counter Russian attacks, highlighting how Kyiv helped defend U.S. bases from Iranian drones. RussiasaidUkrainian drones hit a nuclear plant. Ukraine denied it.

  • 🇨🇴

    Colombia votes:In the first round of voting in the presidential election, leftist Iván Cespeda and far-right, pro-Trump lawyer Abelardo de la Espriellaadvancedto a run-off later in June.

  • 🇨🇩🇺🇬

    Ebola update:As its chief, Tedros Anhanom Ghebreyesus, visited a new Ebola treatment center in DR Congo, the World Health Organizationreported134 cases in Congo and Uganda, resulting in 18 confirmed deaths.


Extraordinary video shot from various angles spread on social media, showing a horribly tense standoff near the Delaney Hall ICE facility in New Jersey. Amid protests, an injured woman in a wheelchair, loudly identifying herself as from the Associated Press and begging to be allowed simply to seek medical help, sought to get through a line of masked agents, all in full riot gear. This is Trump’s America.

  • Ken Paxton,the Republican Senate candidate in Texas, spoke toFox Business. Trotting out familiar attacks on DemocratJames Talerico, including for having “vegan campaign views,” the state AG also gave Talerico a freebie. Asked about being described as “scandal-plagued,” the indicted and impeached Paxton said: “The reality is they can say the same thing aboutDonald Trump.” Well… yeah.

  • Ex-VP

    Mike Pencetold CBSthat Trump’s DOJ “Anti-Weaponization Fund” should be dropped, andtold NBC: “It’s deeply offensive to me that you could… even possibly compensate people who assaulted police officers or vandalized the Capitol on January 6th.” True, some such rioters wanted to hang Pence. Also true, Pencehas a book outin which he cowers before Trump, praising his “conservative principles.”

  • Democratic Rep.

    Ro Khannasaid ChairJames Comerhad made the House OversightJeffrey Epsteininvestigation “totally partisan,”telling MeidasTouch: “He used two, three months to go on a witch hunt forthe Clintons… and yet he didn’t havePam Bondiunder oath, he didn’t have Pam Bondi’s testimony public, he did not have[Howard] Lutnickunder oath, he did not have Lutnick’s testimony public, he has not called the people… in the Justice Department to explain why there have been no prosecutions.”
  • Monday, June 1:Former county clerk Tina Peters, jailed for plotting to tamper with voting machines in support of Trump’s 2020 election subversion but granted clemency by Colorado Governor Jared Polis, is set to befreedfrom prison.
  • Tuesday, June 2:California, Iowa, Montana, New Mexico, New Jersey, and South Dakota will hold primaries. In California, all eyes will be on the race to succeed Gavin Newsom as governor. Democrats Xavier Becerra and Tom Steyer arefavoredto make a run-off, though Republican Steve Hilton is still in the running.
  • Wednesday, June 3:Secretary of State Marco Rubio willappearbefore the Senate Appropriations Committee.
  • Friday, June 5:The U.S. Bureau of Labor Statistics will releasejobs numbers for May, a fiercely scrutinized pointer to how Trump’s economy is or isn’t performing.

Zeteo’s Martin Pengelly contributed to this newsletter.

Still want to get Zeteo content, but not this newsletter? You can unsubscribe directly from ‘First Draft’ by going to ‘

If you enjoy Zeteo, share it with your friends and earn rewards when they subscribe.
